passage: Small cell lung carcinoma (SCLC) is a highly aggressive subtype of lung cancer, strongly associated with smoking and accounting for approximately 15% of all lung cancers. Histologically, it arises from neuroendocrine cells and is characterized by rapid growth, early metastasis, and frequent paraneoplastic syndromes. Diagnosis is often made through imaging, such as chest radiography showing a solitary pulmonary nodule, and confirmed via biopsy. Because SCLC cells contain dense-core neurosecretory granules, they produce several biomarkers detectable in serum. Among these, neuron-specific enolase (NSE) is the most well-established and widely used tumor marker for SCLC. NSE is an isoenzyme of the glycolytic enzyme enolase, predominantly found in neurons and neuroendocrine cells. Elevated serum levels of NSE are seen in the majority of patients with SCLC, correlating with tumor burden and response to therapy. Other markers, such as pro-gastrin-releasing peptide and calcitonin, may also be elevated but are less specific. In contrast, Bence-Jones protein is associated with multiple myeloma, and CA 15-3 is a marker for breast cancer. Therefore, in a patient with confirmed small cell carcinoma, an elevated serum NSE level is the expected laboratory finding.

question:
A 41-year-old man presents to his primary care provider with abdominal pain. He says that the pain “comes and goes” throughout the day and usually lasts 20-30 minutes per episode. He can point to the spot 1-2 inches above the umbilicus where he feels the pain. He denies any feeling of regurgitation or nighttime cough but endorses nausea. He reports that he used to eat three large meals per day but has found that eating more frequently improves his pain. He tried a couple pills of ibuprofen with food over the past couple days and thinks it helped. He has gained four pounds since his past appointment three months ago. The patient denies any diarrhea or change in his stools. He has no past medical history. He drinks 5-6 beers on the weekend and has a 20 pack-year smoking history. He denies any family history of cancer. On physical exam, he is tender to palpation above the umbilicus. Bowel sounds are present. A stool guaiac test is positive. The patient undergoes endoscopy with biopsy to diagnose his condition.

Which of the following is most likely to be found on histology?

options.B: Urease-producing organism in the small intestine
options.C: PAS-positive material in the small intestine
options.D: Crypt abscesses in the large intestine

passage: Peptic ulcer disease is a common condition characterized by mucosal defects in the stomach or duodenum, often presenting with episodic epigastric pain that is relieved by food in duodenal ulcers. Key risk factors include *Helicobacter pylori* infection, nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug use, smoking, and alcohol consumption. *H. pylori* is a gram-negative, spiral-shaped bacterium that produces the enzyme urease, which hydrolyzes urea to ammonia, neutralizing gastric acid and enabling survival in the acidic environment. The organism typically colonizes the gastric antrum but can also be found in areas of gastric metaplasia within the duodenal bulb. On histologic examination of endoscopic biopsy specimens, the bacteria can be identified using special stains such as Giemsa, silver stains, or immunohistochemistry. Additionally, a rapid urease test performed on biopsy tissue can detect the organism’s urease activity. Chronic active gastritis, with infiltration of neutrophils and mononuclear cells, is typically seen in the adjacent mucosa. The presence of urease-producing *H. pylori* is a hallmark finding in duodenal ulcer disease and is central to its pathogenesis and treatment.

question:
A 5-year-old female presents to the pediatrician for a routine office visit. The patient is in kindergarten and doing well in school. She is learning to read and is able to write her first name. Her teacher has no concerns. The patient’s mother is concerned that the patient is a picky eater and often returns home from school with most of her packed lunch uneaten. The patient’s past medical history is significant for moderate persistent asthma, which has required three separate week-long courses of prednisone over the last year and recently diagnosed myopia. The patient’s mother is 5’7”, and the patient’s father is 5’10”. The patient’s weight and height are in the 55th and 5th percentile, respectively, which is consistent with her growth curve. On physical exam, the patient has a low hairline and a broad chest. Her lungs are clear with a mild expiratory wheeze. The patient’s abdomen is soft, non-tender, and non-distended. She has Tanner stage I breast development and pubic hair.

This patient is most likely to have which of the following additional findings?

options.A: Absent Barr bodies on buccal smear

passage: Turner syndrome is a chromosomal disorder resulting from the complete or partial absence of one X chromosome, typically with a 45,X karyotype. Affected individuals are phenotypic females who often present with short stature, a low posterior hairline, a broad chest with widely spaced nipples, and webbed neck. Growth failure is a hallmark, with height often falling below the third percentile by early childhood. In the prepubertal period, ovarian dysgenesis leads to streak gonads and delayed puberty, with Tanner stage I breast development and sparse pubic hair. Other common findings include cubitus valgus, lymphedema of the hands and feet at birth, and an increased risk for congenital heart defects such as coarctation of the aorta. On buccal smear, the absence of a Barr body is characteristic due to the presence of only one X chromosome, which is inactivated as a single copy. This contrasts with normal females, who have two X chromosomes and therefore one Barr body. Turner syndrome should be suspected in girls with unexplained short stature regardless of other typical physical features.

question: A 61-year-old man was started on rosuvastatin 40 mg 8 weeks ago and presents today for a follow-up. He complains of pain in his legs and general weakness. On physical exam, he has full range of motion of his extremities but complains of pain. His blood pressure is 126/84 mm Hg and heart rate is 74/min. The decision is made to stop the statin and return to the clinic in 2 weeks to assess any changes in symptoms. After stopping the statin, his muscular symptoms resolve. What is the next best course of action to take regarding his LDL control?

options.A: Restart rosuvastatin at a lower dose

passage: Statins, such as rosuvastatin, are first-line agents for lowering low-density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and reducing cardiovascular risk. A common adverse effect is statin-associated muscle symptoms (SAMS), which may present as myalgia, weakness, or cramps without significant creatine kinase elevation. If muscle symptoms develop and resolve after discontinuation of the statin, the diagnosis of SAMS is supported. Current guidelines recommend that for patients who experience tolerable muscle symptoms with a statin, the next appropriate step is to restart the same statin at a lower dose. This approach often allows for continued LDL control while mitigating adverse effects, as lower doses are generally associated with fewer muscle complaints. If symptoms recur at the reduced dose, alternative strategies include trying a different statin (e.g., atorvastatin or pravastatin), using alternate-day dosing, or considering non-statin lipid-lowering therapies such as ezetimibe or bempedoic acid. However, simply switching to a different statin or initiating fibrates, fish oils, or other agents is not the first step after successful resolution of symptoms with drug cessation; rather, rechallenge with a lower dose of the original statin is recommended to optimize LDL reduction while maintaining tolerability.

question: An unidentified surgical specimen is received for histopathologic analysis. A portion of the specimen is cut and stained with hematoxylin and eosin. The remainder is analyzed and is found to contains type II collagen and chondroitin sulfate. Which of the following structures is most likely the origin of this surgical specimen?
passage: Cartilage is a specialized connective tissue composed of chondrocytes embedded in an extracellular matrix rich in collagen and proteoglycans. Hyaline cartilage, the most common type, contains type II collagen and the glycosaminoglycan chondroitin sulfate, which is a key component of the proteoglycan aggrecan. This cartilage provides structure and support in various locations, including the larynx, where it forms the thyroid, cricoid, and arytenoid cartilages. In contrast, elastic cartilage (found in the pinna) also contains type II collagen but additionally has abundant elastic fibers; the lens is an avascular structure composed of crystallin proteins, not cartilage; and blood vessel walls contain smooth muscle and elastin. Thus, a surgical specimen yielding type II collagen and chondroitin sulfate is most consistent with hyaline cartilage, which is prominently present in the larynx.

question:
A 70-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department for the evaluation of abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting for 1 day. Computed tomography shows a small bowel perforation. The patient is prepared for emergent exploratory laparotomy. She is sedated with midazolam, induced with propofol, intubated, and maintained on nitrous oxide and isoflurane for the duration of the surgery. A single perforation in the terminal ileum is diagnosed intraoperatively and successfully repaired. The patient is transferred to the intensive care unit. The ventilator is set at an FiO2 of 50%, tidal volume of 1000 mL, respiratory rate of 12/min, and positive end-expiratory pressure of 2.5 cm H2O. Her temperature is 37.3°C (99.1°F), pulse is 76/min, and blood pressure is 111/50 mm Hg. She is responsive to painful stimuli. Lung examination shows bilateral rales. Abdominal examination shows a distended abdomen and intact abdominal surgical incisions. The remainder of the physical examination shows no abnormalities. Arterial blood gas analysis shows:
pH 7.44
pO2 54 mm Hg
pCO2 31 mm Hg
HCO3- 22 mm Hg
Which of the following is the best next step in the management of this patient?"
passage: In the postoperative period, hypoxemia often results from ventilation-perfusion mismatch or intrapulmonary shunting, commonly due to atelectasis, pulmonary edema, or pneumonia. Arterial blood gas analysis reveals a low partial pressure of oxygen (PaO₂) relative to the fraction of inspired oxygen (FiO₂), indicating impaired gas exchange. The alveolar-arterial (A-a) gradient is typically widened. Positive end-expiratory pressure (PEEP) is a mechanical ventilator setting that maintains positive airway pressure at the end of expiration, preventing alveolar collapse and recruiting collapsed alveoli. By increasing functional residual capacity and reducing intrapulmonary shunting, PEEP improves oxygenation. Simply raising FiO₂ may not correct hypoxemia caused by shunt because oxygen cannot reach collapsed or fluid-filled alveoli. Increasing tidal volume or respiratory rate can lead to barotrauma, volutrauma, or respiratory alkalosis, especially when minute ventilation already results in low PaCO₂. In patients with bilateral rales, elevated PEEP can help reopen dependent lung units, reduce edema, and enhance oxygen delivery without exacerbating lung injury. The goal is to optimize oxygenation while limiting oxygen toxicity and ventilator-induced lung injury.

question: A 43-year-old man with a history of hepatitis C and current intravenous drug use presents with 5 days of fever, chills, headache, and severe back pain. On physical exam, temperature is 100.6 deg F (38.1 deg C), blood pressure is 109/56 mmHg, pulse is 94/min, and respirations are 18/min. He is thin and diaphoretic with pinpoint pupils, poor dentition, and track marks on his arms and legs. A high-pitched systolic murmur is heard, loudest in the left sternal border and with inspiration. He is admitted to the hospital and started on broad-spectrum antibiotics. One of the blood cultures drawn 12 hours ago returns positive for Staphylococcus aureus. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to confirm the diagnosis?

options.B: Repeat blood cultures 24 hours after initial cultures were drawn
options.C: Repeat blood cultures 48 hours after initial cultures were drawn

passage: Infective endocarditis (IE) is a serious infection of the endocardial surface of the heart, most commonly involving the valves. Intravenous drug users are at high risk for right-sided IE, frequently caused by *Staphylococcus aureus*. Typical presenting features include fever, chills, and a new or changing heart murmur; in tricuspid valve endocarditis, the murmur may intensify with inspiration (Carvallo’s sign). Diagnosis relies on the modified Duke criteria, which require evidence of persistent bacteremia. For patients with suspected IE, two or more separate blood cultures should be obtained within the first 1–2 hours of evaluation. If initial cultures are positive, repeat blood cultures are indicated to confirm continuous bacteremia—a major criterion. Even after antibiotics are initiated, repeat cultures immediately (not after a fixed delay) help document ongoing infection and guide therapy. Delaying repeat cultures by 24 or 48 hours can mask transient clearance or provide misleading intervals, whereas immediate repeat cultures yield timely confirmation of the diagnosis.

question: A 42-year-old man with AIDS comes to the physician for intermittent fever, nonproductive cough, malaise, decreased appetite, abdominal pain, and a 3.6-kg (8-lb) weight loss over the past month. He has not seen a doctor since he became uninsured 2 years ago. His temperature is 38.3°C (100.9°F). Abdominal examination shows mild, diffuse tenderness throughout the lower quadrants. The liver is palpated 2–3 cm below the right costal margin, and the spleen is palpated 1–2 cm below the left costal margin. His CD4+ T-lymphocyte count is 49/mm3 (N ≥ 500 mm3). Blood cultures grow acid-fast organisms. A PPD skin test shows 4 mm of induration. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy for this patient's condition?
passage: Disseminated *Mycobacterium avium* complex (MAC) infection is a frequent opportunistic complication in patients with advanced HIV/AIDS, especially when the CD4+ T-lymphocyte count drops below 50 cells/mm³. Presenting features include persistent fever, night sweats, weight loss, malaise, nonproductive cough, abdominal pain, and hepatosplenomegaly. Blood cultures typically reveal acid-fast bacilli, confirming the diagnosis. Tuberculin skin testing (PPD) often shows less than 5 mm of induration due to anergy in immunocompromised individuals, making it unreliable for distinguishing MAC from tuberculosis. Standard initial pharmacotherapy for disseminated MAC consists of a macrolide antibiotic (azithromycin or clarithromycin) combined with ethambutol. A third agent, such as rifabutin or an amikacin, may be added in cases of severe disease or suspected drug resistance. Concurrent initiation of antiretroviral therapy is essential for immune restoration and long-term infection control.

question: A 49-year-old woman comes to the physician for a scheduled colposcopy. Two weeks ago, she had a routine Pap smear that showed atypical squamous cells. Colposcopy shows an area of white discoloration of the cervix with application of acetic acid solution. Biopsy of this area shows carcinoma-in-situ. Activation of which of the following best explains the pathogenesis of this condition?
passage: Cervical carcinoma-in-situ, a preinvasive neoplasm, arises primarily from persistent infection with high-risk human papillomavirus (HPV) types. The viral oncoproteins E6 and E7 drive malignant transformation by disrupting cell cycle regulation. Specifically, the E7 protein binds to and inactivates the retinoblastoma protein (pRb), a tumor suppressor that normally sequesters E2F transcription factors. When pRb is inhibited, E2F factors are released and become constitutively active, promoting transcription of genes required for S-phase entry and cell proliferation. This unscheduled cell cycle progression leads to genomic instability, clonal expansion of abnormal cells, and the development of dysplasia. Over time, this results in full-thickness epithelial involvement without stromal invasion, defined as carcinoma-in-situ. In contrast, HPV E6 promotes degradation of p53, impairing apoptosis and DNA repair, but the direct activation of E2F transcription factors via pRb inactivation is a critical early step in the pathogenesis of cervical intraepithelial neoplasia. Thus, dysregulated E2F activity is central to the proliferative drive underlying carcinoma-in-situ.

question: A research group has developed a low-cost diagnostic retinal imaging device for cytomegalovirus retinitis in a population of HIV-infected patients. In a pilot study of 50 patients, the imaging test detected the presence of CMV retinitis in 50% of the patients. An ophthalmologist slit lamp examination, which was performed for each patient to serve as the gold standard for diagnosis, confirmed a diagnosis of CMV retinitis in 20 patients that were found to be positive through imaging, as well as 1 patient who tested negative with the device. If the prevalence of CMV retinitis in the population decreases due to increased access to antiretroviral therapy, how will positive predictive value and negative predictive value of the diagnostic test be affected?
passage: In diagnostic testing, positive predictive value (PPV) and negative predictive value (NPV) describe the probability that a positive or negative test result correctly reflects the true disease status. These metrics are not intrinsic properties of a test; they depend heavily on the prevalence of the disease in the tested population. Prevalence is the proportion of individuals in a population who actually have the condition at a given time. When prevalence decreases—for example, due to effective interventions like antiretroviral therapy reducing the occurrence of cytomegalovirus retinitis in HIV-infected patients—the PPV of a diagnostic test typically decreases, while the NPV increases. This occurs because a lower prevalence reduces the number of true positives relative to false positives, making a positive result less reliable. Conversely, the proportion of true negatives among those who test negative rises, enhancing the confidence that a negative result truly indicates absence of disease. Sensitivity and specificity, which measure a test’s ability to correctly identify diseased and non-diseased individuals respectively, remain constant regardless of prevalence.

question: A 35-year-old woman is brought into the clinic by a concerned neighbor who says that the patient is often seen setting up bear traps all around her property because of an impending ‘invasion of the mole people.’ The patient has come to the clinic wearing a garlic necklace. She vaguely explains that the necklace is to mask her scent from the moles tracking her. She has no past psychiatric history and she denies hearing voices or seeing objects. No significant past medical history. Although she has lived in the same community for years, she says she usually keeps to herself and does not have many friends. She holds a regular job at the local hardware store and lives alone. Which of the following is the best initial course of treatment for this patient?

options.B: The patient does not require any intervention

passage: Delusional disorder is characterized by the presence of one or more non-bizarre delusions that persist for at least one month, without prominent hallucinations, disorganized speech, or negative symptoms of schizophrenia. Individuals with this condition often function reasonably well in daily life, maintaining employment and self-care, but may exhibit social isolation or eccentric behaviors related to their fixed false beliefs. Common themes include persecutory, jealous, somatic, or grandiose delusions. Unlike schizophrenia, hallucinations are not prominent, if present at all. Because patients typically have intact reality testing outside the delusion and may lack insight into the irrationality of the belief, treatment often focuses on engagement and trust-building. Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) is considered a first-line psychosocial intervention for delusional disorder, as it helps patients gradually examine evidence for and against their beliefs without direct confrontation, reduce distress, and improve functioning. Antipsychotic medication may be used secondarily, particularly for severe or refractory cases, but psychotherapy is prioritized in higher-functioning patients who do not pose imminent risk to themselves or others. Group therapy and electroconvulsive therapy are not standard initial treatments for this condition.

question: A 71-year-old man complains of urinary hesitancy and nocturia of increasing frequency over the past several months. Digital rectal exam was positive for a slightly enlarged prostate but did not detect any additional abnormalities of the prostate or rectum. The patient’s serum PSA was measured to be 6 ng/mL. Image A shows a transabdominal ultrasound of the patient. Which of the following medications should be included to optimally treat the patient's condition?
passage: Benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) is a common condition in aging men, characterized by nonmalignant enlargement of the prostate gland. Symptoms arise from bladder outlet obstruction and include urinary hesitancy, weak stream, frequency, urgency, and nocturia. Diagnosis is typically based on clinical history, digital rectal exam (DRE), and serum prostate-specific antigen (PSA) levels. In BPH, the prostate is symmetrically enlarged and smooth on DRE, while PSA may be mildly elevated (typically 4–10 ng/mL). Transabdominal ultrasound can assess prostate volume and postvoid residual urine. First-line medical therapy for BPH includes alpha-1 adrenergic receptor antagonists (e.g., tamsulosin) to relax smooth muscle in the prostate and bladder neck, and 5α-reductase inhibitors such as finasteride. Finasteride blocks the conversion of testosterone to dihydrotestosterone, reducing prostate size over months and improving urinary symptoms, particularly in men with moderate-to-severe enlargement. It also lowers serum PSA levels by approximately 50%. This medication is indicated for symptomatic BPH with an enlarged prostate, as seen in this patient.

question: A 30-year-old man presents to his primary care provider complaining of drowsiness at work for the past several months. He finds his work as a computer programmer rewarding and looks forward to coming into the office every day. However, he often falls asleep during meetings even though he usually gets a good night sleep and has limited his exposure to alcohol and caffeine in the evening. His past medical history is noncontributory. His vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination is unremarkable. The primary care provider recommends keeping a sleep journal and provides a questionnaire for the patient’s wife. The patient returns one month later to report no changes to his condition. The sleep journal reveals that the patient is getting sufficient sleep and wakes up rested. The questionnaire reveals that the patient does not snore nor does he stop breathing during his sleep. A sleep study reveals mean sleep latency of 6 minutes. Which of the following is the best course of treatment for this patient’s condition?
passage: Narcolepsy is a chronic neurological disorder characterized by excessive daytime sleepiness (EDS) and a tendency to fall asleep suddenly, even after sufficient nocturnal sleep. The condition often emerges in young adulthood and can significantly impair occupational and social functioning. Diagnosis typically involves a multiple sleep latency test (MSLT), where a mean sleep latency of less than 8 minutes (often with two or more sleep-onset rapid eye movement periods) confirms pathological sleepiness. Key differentials include sleep apnea and insufficient sleep syndrome, which can be ruled out through history, sleep journals, and partner questionnaires documenting absence of snoring or breathing pauses. First-line pharmacotherapy for narcolepsy-associated EDS involves central nervous system stimulants such as methylphenidate or modafinil. These agents promote wakefulness by enhancing dopaminergic and noradrenergic signaling. Sodium oxybate is reserved for cataplexy or refractory cases, while tricyclic antidepressants like imipramine are used primarily for cataplexy rather than EDS. Scheduled daytime naps may be beneficial as adjunctive therapy but are rarely sufficient as monotherapy for moderate to severe sleepiness.

question: A 40-year-old man comes to the physician for the evaluation of a painless right-sided scrotal swelling. The patient reports that he first noticed the swelling several weeks ago, but it is not always present. He has hypertension treated with enalapril. His father was diagnosed with a seminoma at the age of 25 years. The patient has smoked a pack of cigarettes daily for the past 20 years. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows a 10-cm, soft, cystic, nontender right scrotal mass that transilluminates. The mass does not increase in size on coughing and it is possible to palpate normal tissue above the mass. There are no bowel sounds in the mass, and it does not reduce when the patient is in a supine position. Examination of the testis shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely cause of the mass?

options.D: Imbalance of fluid secretion and resorption by the tunica vaginalis

passage: A hydrocele is an accumulation of serous fluid within the tunica vaginalis, the peritoneal remnant that envelops the testis. This condition typically presents as a painless, cystic scrotal swelling that transilluminates and does not enlarge with coughing or Valsalva maneuver. The testis is usually palpable separately from the mass, and the swelling often persists unchanged when the patient lies supine. In adults, most hydroceles are primary, arising from an imbalance between fluid secretion and absorption by the tunica vaginalis, leading to gradual accumulation. This contrasts with a communicating hydrocele, which results from a patent processus vaginalis and is more common in infants. Other scrotal masses, such as varicoceles (dilation of the pampiniform plexus) or indirect inguinal hernias, exhibit distinct features—varicoceles feel like a “bag of worms” and are accentuated by standing or coughing, while hernias may contain bowel sounds and are reducible. A hydrocele is not caused by torsion of the spermatic cord, which produces acute pain and tenderness. Diagnosis is often clinical, and treatment may involve aspiration or surgical repair if symptomatic.

question: A 62-year-old man presents to the physician because of incomplete healing of a chest wound. He recently had a triple coronary artery bypass graft 3 weeks ago. His past medical history is significant for type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension for the past 25 years. Clinical examination shows the presence of wound dehiscence in the lower 3rd of the sternal region. The wound surface shows the presence of dead necrotic tissue with pus. Computed tomography (CT) of the thorax shows a small fluid collection with fat stranding in the perisurgical soft tissues. What is the most appropriate next step in the management of the patient?
passage: Post-sternotomy wound complications, including dehiscence and deep sternal infection, are serious sequelae following cardiac surgery, particularly in patients with diabetes mellitus and other comorbidities. Clinical features such as wound separation, necrotic tissue, purulent drainage, and perisurgical fluid collections with fat stranding on imaging indicate infection and tissue devitalization. The cornerstone of initial management is surgical debridement, which entails systematic removal of all nonviable, infected, and necrotic tissue, including bone, cartilage, and soft tissue. This step is essential to eliminate the bacterial burden and establish a clean wound bed. Negative pressure wound therapy may be used as an adjunct following debridement to promote granulation and facilitate closure, but it should not replace primary debridement in the presence of gross infection and necrotic debris. Sternal rewiring or fixation is typically deferred until infection is controlled, as placing hardware into an infected field risks persistent osteomyelitis and failure of repair. Therefore, in patients presenting with infected sternal wound dehiscence and evidence of deep soft-tissue infection, immediate surgical debridement is the appropriate next step.

question: The Kozak sequence for the Beta-globin gene has a known mutation which decreases, though does not abolish, translation of the Beta-globin mRNA, leading to a phenotype of thalassemia intermedia. What would the blood smear be expected to show in a patient positive for this mutation?
passage: The beta-globin gene contains a conserved Kozak consensus sequence surrounding the start codon, which is critical for efficient translation initiation. A specific mutation within this sequence reduces the affinity of the ribosomal preinitiation complex for the mRNA, leading to decreased, but not absent, translation of beta-globin. This partial deficit in beta-globin chain synthesis results in an unbalanced accumulation of alpha-globin chains, which precipitate within erythroid precursors and mature red blood cells. The consequent ineffective erythropoiesis and hemolysis produce a chronic anemia classified as thalassemia intermedia. In the peripheral blood smear, the reduced hemoglobinization of erythrocytes leads to a population of cells that are smaller than normal, or microcytic, often accompanied by hypochromia. This microcytosis is a hallmark of beta-thalassemia syndromes, reflecting the impaired hemoglobin synthesis. Unlike conditions with macrocytosis (e.g., megaloblastic anemia) or hyperchromia, the decreased mean corpuscular volume in thalassemia intermedia correlates directly with the severity of the globin chain imbalance. Additional findings may include target cells and basophilic stippling, but microcytic red blood cells dominate the smear.

question: A 68-year-old man from California presents for a routine checkup. He does not have any complaints. He says that he has tried to keep himself healthy and active by jogging and gardening since his retirement at age 50. He adds that he spends his mornings in the park and his afternoons in his garden. He has no significant medical history. The patient denies any smoking history and drinks alcohol occasionally. On physical examination, the following lesion is seen on his scalp (see image). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
passage: Actinic keratosis is a common, premalignant skin lesion that arises from prolonged cumulative exposure to ultraviolet radiation. It typically presents as a rough, scaly, erythematous patch or papule on sun-exposed areas such as the scalp, face, ears, forearms, and dorsum of the hands. Older adults, especially those with fair skin and a history of outdoor activities, are at highest risk. The lesions are often more easily felt than seen, with a sandpaper-like texture. While actinic keratoses are benign, they have the potential to progress to squamous cell carcinoma if left untreated, with an estimated conversion rate of less than 1% per year per lesion. Diagnosis is primarily clinical, though biopsy may be performed if there is concern for malignancy. Management options include cryotherapy, topical chemotherapeutic agents, photodynamic therapy, and regular monitoring. Prevention involves sun protection measures such as sunscreen, hats, and protective clothing. Because actinic keratoses reflect significant cumulative sun damage, patients with multiple lesions warrant periodic skin examinations to detect early malignant transformation.

question: A routine newborn screening test for phenylketonuria in a male neonate shows a serum phenylalanine concentration of 44 mg/dL (N < 20). He is started on a special diet and the hyperphenylalaninemia resolves. At a routine well-child examination 4 months later, the physician notices that he has persistent head lag. On examination, he has blue eyes, pale skin, blonde hair, and generalized hypotonia. His serum prolactin level is markedly elevated. Supplementation of which of the following substances is most likely to prevent further complications of this patient's condition?
passage: Phenylketonuria (PKU) is an inherited disorder of phenylalanine metabolism, most commonly caused by a deficiency of phenylalanine hydroxylase (PAH). However, a distinct subset of patients exhibit hyperphenylalaninemia due to defects in the synthesis or recycling of tetrahydrobiopterin (BH4), an essential cofactor for PAH as well as for tyrosine hydroxylase and tryptophan hydroxylase. Unlike classic PKU, BH4 deficiencies impair not only phenylalanine catabolism but also the production of dopamine and serotonin. Affected infants typically present with elevated serum phenylalanine on newborn screening, which may initially respond to dietary phenylalanine restriction. However, despite normalization of phenylalanine levels, neurological deterioration—such as persistent head lag, hypotonia, and developmental delay—can emerge within months. Characteristic physical findings include fair skin, blue eyes, and blonde hair due to impaired melanin synthesis. A hallmark biochemical clue is markedly elevated serum prolactin, resulting from reduced dopaminergic inhibition of prolactin secretion. Management of BH4 deficiency requires supplementation with tetrahydrobiopterin itself, as dietary phenylalanine restriction alone cannot correct the neurotransmitter deficits. Early BH4 therapy can prevent irreversible neurological damage, whereas tyrosine, pyridoxine, or thiamine are not effective in addressing the underlying cofactor defect.

question: A 36-year-old nursing home worker presents to the clinic with the complaints of breathlessness, cough, and night sweats for the past 2 months. She further expresses her concerns about the possibility of contracting tuberculosis as one of the patients under her care is being treated for tuberculosis. A PPD skin test is done and reads 11 mm on day 3. Chest X-ray demonstrates a cavitary lesion in the right upper lobe. The standard anti-tuberculosis medication regimen is started. At a follow-up appointment 3 months later the patient presents with fatigue. She has also been experiencing occasional dizziness, weakness, and numbness in her feet. Physical exam is positive for conjunctival pallor. Lab work is significant for a hemoglobin level of 10 g/dL and mean corpuscular volume of 68 fl. What is the most likely cause of her current symptoms?
passage: Isoniazid, a first-line agent in the standard four-drug regimen for tuberculosis, interferes with pyridoxine (vitamin B6) metabolism by forming a hydrazone complex that inhibits pyridoxal kinase. Pyridoxal phosphate, the active form of vitamin B6, serves as an essential cofactor for aminolevulinic acid (ALA) synthase—the rate-limiting enzyme in heme biosynthesis. Inhibition of this enzyme reduces ALA production, thereby impairing heme synthesis. The resulting sideroblastic anemia is typically microcytic and hypochromic, with low mean corpuscular volume and hemoglobin levels. Clinically, this manifests as fatigue, pallor, and weakness. Additionally, pyridoxine deficiency disrupts neurotransmitter synthesis and myelin formation, leading to peripheral neuropathy—characterized by paresthesias, numbness, and weakness in the extremities. Conjunctival pallor and the laboratory findings of a hemoglobin of 10 g/dL with an MCV of 68 fL in a patient receiving isoniazid are consistent with decreased ALA synthase activity due to drug-induced pyridoxine depletion. Routine supplementation of pyridoxine is recommended during isoniazid therapy to prevent these adverse effects.

question: A 23-year-old woman is referred to a genetic counselor. She is feeling well but is concerned because her brother was recently diagnosed with hereditary hemochromatosis. All first-degree relatives were encouraged to undergo genetic screening for any mutations associated with the disease. Today, she denies fever, chills, joint pain, or skin hyperpigmentation. Her temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 85/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 123/78 mm Hg. Her physical examination is normal. Her serum iron, hemoglobin, ferritin, and AST and ALT concentrations are normal. Gene screening will involve a blood specimen. Which of the following genes would suggest hereditary hemochromatosis?
passage: Hereditary hemochromatosis is an autosomal recessive disorder characterized by excessive absorption of dietary iron, leading to pathological iron accumulation in organs such as the liver, heart, pancreas, and joints. The vast majority of cases are caused by mutations in the HFE gene, located on chromosome 6. The most common pathogenic variant is C282Y, with a less common variant being H63D; homozygosity for C282Y accounts for most clinical presentations. Penetrance is incomplete, meaning not all individuals with the genotype develop symptomatic disease, and iron overload often manifests in mid-adulthood. Because first-degree relatives of an affected individual have a 25% chance of being homozygous if both parents are carriers, genetic screening is recommended to identify at-risk family members before iron accumulation occurs. Serum iron studies, including ferritin and transferrin saturation, as well as liver enzyme levels, are typically normal in early stages. Thus, genetic testing for HFE mutations is the definitive diagnostic approach for presymptomatic detection and counseling.

question:
A 16-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department with constant abdominal pain over the past 8 hours. The pain is in her right lower quadrant (RLQ), which is also where it began. She has had no nausea or vomiting despite eating a snack 2 hours ago. She had a similar episode last month which resolved on its own. Her menstrual cycles are 28–30 days apart with 3–5 days of vaginal bleeding. Her last menses ended 9 days ago. Her blood pressure is 125/75 mm Hg, the pulse is 78/min, the respirations are 15/min, and the temperature is 37.2°C (99.0°F). Abdominal examination shows moderate pain on direct pressure over the RLQ which decreases with the release of pressure. The remainder of the physical examination shows no abnormalities. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin 12.5 mg/dL
Leukocyte count 6000/mm3
Segmented neutrophils 55%
Lymphocytes 39%
Platelet count 260,000/mm3
Serum  
C-reactive protein 5 mg/L (N < 8 mg/L)
Urine  
RBC 1-2 phf
WBC None
Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
passage: Right lower quadrant abdominal pain in an adolescent female often raises concern for appendicitis, but several gynecologic and benign causes must be considered. Mid-cycle pain, or mittelschmerz, occurs around ovulation when follicular rupture irritates the peritoneum, typically presenting as transient, unilateral lower abdominal pain that may recur monthly. The pain is usually self-limited, with no accompanying fever, nausea, or significant leukocytosis. Physical examination may reveal mild tenderness without rebound or guarding. Laboratory studies, including white blood cell count and C-reactive protein, remain within normal limits. Urinalysis is unremarkable. The menstrual history is crucial: pain near the midpoint of a regular cycle (approximately day 14 in a 28-day cycle) supports mittelschmerz. In contrast, appendicitis often presents with progressive pain, anorexia, nausea, fever, and peritoneal signs such as rebound tenderness. Ovarian torsion or ruptured ovarian cyst may cause acute, severe pain with possible vomiting, while ectopic pregnancy must be excluded in sexually active patients by pregnancy testing and menstrual history. In a hemodynamically stable adolescent with characteristic mid-cycle pain, normal vital signs, and unremarkable labs, careful observation and reassurance are appropriate, as the condition typically resolves without intervention. Surgical referral or pharmacologic treatment is not indicated.

question: A 30-year-old male presents to the emergency department with a complaint of abdominal pain. The patient states he was at a barbecue around noon. Lunch was arranged as a buffet without refrigeration. Within 2 to 3 hours, he had abdominal pain with 3 episodes of watery diarrhea. On exam, vital signs are T 99.1, HR 103, BP 110/55, RR 14. Abdominal exam is significant for pain to deep palpation without any rebounding or guarding. There is no blood on fecal occult testing (FOBT). What is the most likely cause of this patient's presentation?
passage: Staphylococcal food poisoning is a common form of gastroenteritis caused by ingestion of preformed enterotoxins produced by *Staphylococcus aureus*. The illness is characterized by a remarkably short incubation period, typically 2 to 4 hours after consumption of contaminated food. Symptoms include sudden onset of nausea, vomiting, abdominal cramps, and watery diarrhea. Fever is often absent or low-grade. The source is usually food that has been handled improperly and left at room temperature, allowing the bacteria to multiply and produce toxin. Commonly implicated foods include meats, dairy products, salads, and buffet-style meals that are not adequately refrigerated. The enterotoxin is heat-stable and is not destroyed by cooking. Illness is self-limiting, typically resolving within 24 to 48 hours. Diagnosis is clinical, based on the short incubation period and characteristic symptoms. No blood is present in the stool, distinguishing it from invasive bacterial pathogens. Supportive care with fluid replacement is the mainstay of treatment.

question:
A 70-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by staff of the group home where he resides because of worsening confusion for the past week. He has a history of major depressive disorder and had an ischemic stroke 4 months ago. Current medications are aspirin and sertraline. He is lethargic and disoriented. His pulse is 78/min, and blood pressure is 135/88 mm Hg. Physical examination shows moist oral mucosa, normal skin turgor, and no peripheral edema. While in the waiting room, he has a generalized, tonic-clonic seizure. Laboratory studies show a serum sodium of 119 mEq/L and an elevated serum antidiuretic hormone concentration. Which of the following sets of additional laboratory findings is most likely in this patient?
 $$$ Serum osmolality %%% Urine sodium %%% Serum aldosterone $$$
passage: In euvolemic hyponatremia, such as that seen in the syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion (SIADH), the elevated antidiuretic hormone (ADH) causes excessive water reabsorption in the collecting ducts, diluting the serum sodium and lowering serum osmolality. The expanded intravascular volume, though clinically inapparent, suppresses the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system, leading to decreased serum aldosterone levels. Because the kidney retains sodium only under appropriate hormonal stimuli, the presence of volume expansion and continued ADH activity results in a high urinary sodium concentration, typically above 40 mEq/L, as the body attempts to excrete the excess sodium load. Conversely, in hypovolemic hyponatremia, both serum osmolality and urine sodium are low, and aldosterone is elevated due to volume contraction. Thus, the combination of low serum osmolality, high urine sodium, and low serum aldosterone is characteristic of SIADH in a euvolemic patient.

question: A 35-year-old woman presents with severe fear reactions to seeing dogs after moving into a new suburban neighborhood. She states that she has always had an irrational and excessive fear of dogs but has been able to avoid it for most of her life while living in the city. When she sees her neighbors walking their dogs outside, she is terrified and begins to feel short of breath. Recently, she has stopped picking up her children from the bus stop and no longer plays outside with her children in order to avoid seeing any dogs. Which of the following would be the best definitive treatment for this patient?
passage: Systematic desensitization is a behavioral therapy technique widely regarded as a definitive treatment for specific phobias, including intense, irrational fears of animals such as dogs. This method, developed by Joseph Wolpe, relies on the principle of reciprocal inhibition: a patient cannot be both relaxed and fearful at the same time. The therapist first teaches the patient relaxation skills, such as deep breathing or progressive muscle relaxation. Then, the patient constructs a fear hierarchy—a ranked list of situations related to the feared object, ranging from least to most anxiety-provoking (e.g., seeing a picture of a dog, hearing a bark, or standing near a leashed dog). Gradually, the patient confronts each step while maintaining a relaxed state, moving up the hierarchy only after mastering the previous level. Over repeated sessions, the fear response diminishes through a process called counterconditioning. Unlike pharmacotherapy (e.g., SSRIs or benzodiazepines), which primarily manage symptoms, systematic desensitization directly addresses the learned avoidance and phobic response, helping patients regain normal functioning—such as walking outdoors or picking up children from a bus stop—without needing lifelong medication.

question: A 45-year-old man arrives by ambulance to the emergency room after being involved in a very severe construction accident. The patient was found unconscious with a large metal spike protruding from his abdomen by a coworker who was unable to estimate the amount of time the patient went without medical aid. Upon arrival to the ER, the patient was unconscious and unresponsive. His vital signs are BP: 80/40, HR: 120 bpm, RR: 25 bpm, Temperature: 97.1 degrees, and SPO2: 99%.He is taken to the operating room to remove the foreign body and control the bleeding. Although both objectives were accomplished, the patient had an acute drop in his blood pressure during the surgery at which time ST elevations were noted in multiple leads. This resolved with adequate fluid resuscitation and numerous blood transfusions. The patient remained sedated after surgery and continued to have relatively stable vital signs until his third day in the intensive care unit, when he experiences an oxygen desaturation of 85% despite being on a respirator with 100% oxygen at 15 breaths/minute. On auscultation air entry is present bilaterally with the presence of crackles. A 2/6 systolic murmur is heard. Readings from a Swan-Ganz catheter display the following: central venous pressure (CVP): 4 mmHg, right ventricular pressure (RVP) 20/5 mmHg, pulmonary artery pressure (PAP): 20/5 mmHg. Pulmonary capillary wedge pressure (PCWP): 5 mm Hg. A chest x-ray is shown as Image A. The patient dies soon after this episode. What is the most likely direct cause of his death?
passage: Diffuse alveolar damage (DAD) is the histopathological hallmark of acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS), a life-threatening form of noncardiogenic pulmonary edema. DAD typically arises from direct or indirect lung injury, with common triggers including severe trauma, hemorrhagic shock, massive blood transfusions, and systemic inflammatory responses. Clinically, ARDS presents as acute-onset hypoxemia refractory to supplemental oxygen, bilateral airspace opacities on chest radiography, and a pulmonary capillary wedge pressure (PCWP) less than 18 mmHg, which distinguishes it from cardiogenic pulmonary edema. The low PCWP, often accompanied by normal or low central venous and pulmonary artery pressures, reflects increased pulmonary vascular permeability rather than elevated left-sided filling pressures. Auscultation reveals bilateral crackles, and a systolic murmur may be present due to hyperdynamic circulation or pre-existing conditions. In severe cases, hypoxemia worsens despite mechanical ventilation with high fractions of inspired oxygen, leading to progressive respiratory failure and death. Histologically, DAD shows hyaline membranes lining alveolar spaces, interstitial and alveolar edema, and later fibrosis. This pattern is distinct from myocardial free wall rupture, ventricular septal defect, or reinfarction, which would typically produce cardiogenic shock with elevated PCWP and distinct hemodynamic profiles.

question: A 76-year-old man with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) presents complaining of 3 weeks of cough and progressive dyspnea on exertion in the setting of a 20 pound weight loss. He is a 60 pack-year smoker, worked as a shipbuilder 30 years ago, and recently traveled to Ohio to visit family. Chest radiograph shows increased bronchovascular markings, reticular parenchymal opacities, and multiple pleural plaques. Labs are unremarkable except for a slight anemia. Which of the following is the most likely finding on this patient's chest CT?

options.A: Nodular mass spreading along pleural surfaces

passage: In patients with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and a significant smoking history, the development of a lower lobe cavitary mass on chest CT is a classic presentation of primary lung cancer, particularly squamous cell carcinoma. Such lesions often arise in the setting of pre-existing pulmonary parenchymal changes, including emphysema and reticular opacities from smoking or occupational exposures. Pleural plaques, as seen on chest radiography, are a hallmark of prior asbestos exposure—common in shipbuilders—and further increase the risk of bronchogenic carcinoma. The presence of unintentional weight loss and progressive dyspnea suggests an advanced malignancy. While a cavitary mass can also occur from granulomatous infections such as histoplasmosis in endemic regions (e.g., Ohio), the combination of extensive smoking history, asbestos-related pleural disease, and COPD makes lung carcinoma the most probable etiology. Lower lobe predominance is typical for many lung cancers, and cavitation results from central necrosis within the tumor. Other imaging findings, such as honeycombing or diffuse pleural nodularity, are more characteristic of idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis or mesothelioma, respectively, but are less consistent with the acute symptom progression and radiographic pattern described.

question: A 60-year-old man presents with a 2-day history of increasing difficulty in breathing with a productive cough. He reports having shortness of breath over the last 6 months, but he has felt worse since he contracted a cold that has been traveling around his office. Today, he reports body aches, headache, and fever along with this chronic cough. His past medical history is significant for prediabetes, which he controls with exercise and diet. He has a 30-pack-year smoking history. His blood pressure is 130/85 mmHg, pulse rate is 90/min, temperature is 36.9°C (98.5°F), and respiratory rate is 18/min. Physical examination reveals diminished breath sounds bilateral, a barrel-shaped chest, and measured breathing through pursed lips. A chest X-ray reveals a flattened diaphragm and no signs of consolidation. Pulmonary function testing reveals FEV1/FVC ratio of 60%. Arterial blood gases (ABG) of this patient are most likely to reveal which of the following?

options.D: Anion gap metabolic acidosis with respiratory alkalosis

passage: In chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), airflow limitation is largely irreversible and progressive, often resulting from cigarette smoke exposure. Pathologically, the disease encompasses emphysema and chronic bronchitis, leading to airway inflammation, alveolar destruction, and loss of elastic recoil. Clinically, patients develop a barrel-shaped chest, pursed-lip breathing, and a flattened diaphragm on chest radiography. Pulmonary function testing confirms obstruction with a reduced FEV₁/FVC ratio, typically below 70%. In stable COPD, arterial blood gases may show chronic respiratory acidosis with renal compensation (elevated bicarbonate). However, during an acute exacerbation—often triggered by infection—hypoventilation worsens, causing a dramatic rise in arterial partial pressure of carbon dioxide (PaCO₂). This acute-on-chronic hypercapnia overwhelms renal compensatory mechanisms, leading to a low pH and elevated PaCO₂, a pattern of primary respiratory acidosis. Symptoms include dyspnea, productive cough, fever, and headache. Unlike metabolic acidoses, the anion gap remains normal, and the primary disturbance is ventilatory failure rather than metabolic derangement. Prompt recognition of acute respiratory acidosis in COPD exacerbations guides appropriate management, including bronchodilators, corticosteroids, and noninvasive ventilation.

question: A 34-year-old male suffers from inherited hemophilia A. He and his wife have three unaffected daughters. What is the probability that the second daughter is a carrier of the disease?
passage: Hemophilia A is an X-linked recessive bleeding disorder caused by mutations in the F8 gene, which encodes coagulation factor VIII. In X-linked recessive inheritance, males have one X chromosome and thus express the disorder if they inherit the mutant allele. Affected males pass their X chromosome to all of their daughters; consequently, every daughter of a man with hemophilia A inherits the mutant X chromosome and is an obligate carrier. The daughters are typically asymptomatic unless they also inherit a mutant allele from their mother, but they will always carry one copy of the mutation. If the mother is unaffected and not a carrier, the daughters cannot be affected and will all be carriers. This pattern is independent of birth order; each daughter has a 100% probability of being a carrier, as she must receive the father’s X chromosome. Sons, in contrast, inherit the father’s Y chromosome and are not carriers. Therefore, in a family where the father has hemophilia A and the mother is a non-carrier, all daughters are obligate carriers, and none of the sons will inherit the condition from the father.

question: A 76-year-old man is brought to his physician's office by his wife due to progressively worsening hearing loss. The patient reports that he noticed a decrease in his hearing approximately 10 years ago. His wife says that he watches television at an elevated volume and appears to have trouble understanding what is being said to him, especially when there is background noise. He states that he also experiences constant ear ringing and episodes of unsteadiness. On physical examination, the outer ears are normal and otoscopic findings are unremarkable. The patient is unable to repeat the sentence said to him on whisper testing. When a vibrating tuning fork is placed in the middle of the patient's forehead, it is heard equally on both ears. When the vibrating tuning fork is placed by the ear and then on the mastoid process, air conduction is greater than bone conduction. Which of the following structures is most likely impaired in this patient?
passage: Age-related hearing loss, known as presbycusis, is a common sensorineural disorder that typically begins in the fifth or sixth decade of life and progresses gradually. It results from degeneration of the cochlear hair cells and auditory nerve fibers, leading to difficulty understanding speech, especially in noisy environments. Patients often report bilateral high-frequency hearing loss, constant tinnitus (ringing in the ears), and sometimes episodes of unsteadiness due to concurrent vestibular dysfunction. On physical examination, the outer ear and tympanic membrane appear normal. Whispers are not heard, confirming reduced auditory acuity. The Weber test, performed by placing a vibrating tuning fork on the forehead, shows equal sound lateralization to both ears in symmetric sensorineural loss. The Rinne test, comparing air conduction (placing the fork near the ear) to bone conduction (on the mastoid), normally demonstrates air conduction greater than bone conduction; this finding persists in sensorineural hearing loss, distinguishing it from conductive impairment. In presbycusis, the primary pathological changes involve the cochlea, particularly the hair cells of the organ of Corti and the stria vascularis, rather than the ossicles or tympanic membrane.

question: A 13-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his mother because of a 6-hour history of severe eye pain and blurry vision. He wears soft contact lenses and has not removed them for 2 days. Ophthalmologic examination shows a deep corneal ulcer, severe conjunctival injection, and purulent discharge on the right. Treatment with topical ciprofloxacin is initiated. A culture of the ocular discharge is most likely to show which of the following?
passage: Bacterial keratitis is a sight-threatening infection of the cornea, most commonly associated with prolonged wear of soft contact lenses. The pathogen most frequently implicated in contact lens-related corneal ulcers is *Pseudomonas aeruginosa*, an aerobic, gram-negative bacillus that is oxidase-positive. This organism thrives in moist environments, such as contact lens cases and solutions, and can adhere to the lens surface, forming biofilms. Infection typically presents with rapid onset of severe eye pain, photophobia, conjunctival injection, purulent discharge, and a characteristic deep corneal ulcer. *P. aeruginosa* produces proteolytic enzymes that can rapidly destroy corneal stroma, necessitating prompt antimicrobial therapy. Topical fluoroquinolones, such as ciprofloxacin, are commonly used as first-line treatment. Microbiological cultures of corneal scrapings or discharge from such infections typically yield gram-negative, oxidase-positive rods, confirming *P. aeruginosa* as the causative agent. Other gram-negative bacilli, such as *Serratia* or *Enterobacter*, are less common, while gram-positive cocci like *Streptococcus pneumoniae* are more often associated with trauma rather than contact lens use.

question: A 31-year-old female patient complains of numbness and tingling in her left hand, weakness, difficulty with walking, dizziness, and bladder dysfunction. She said that about a year ago, she had trouble with her vision, and that it eventually recovered in a few days. On physical exam, bilateral internuclear ophthalmoplegia, hyperreflexia in both patella, and bilateral clonus, are noted. A magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) study was done (Figure 1). If a lumbar puncture is performed in this patient, which of the following would most likely be found in cerebrospinal fluid analysis?
passage: Multiple sclerosis (MS) is a chronic demyelinating disease of the central nervous system characterized by inflammatory lesions that disrupt nerve conduction. Its clinical presentation often involves relapsing-remitting episodes of neurologic deficits, including optic neuritis, sensory disturbances, motor weakness, ataxia, and bladder dysfunction. Internuclear ophthalmoplegia, particularly when bilateral, is a classic sign of brainstem demyelination. Diagnosis typically relies on a combination of clinical history, MRI findings showing disseminated white matter lesions in space and time, and supportive laboratory evidence. Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis is a valuable adjunct; in MS, it commonly reveals elevated IgG levels due to intrathecal synthesis. The presence of oligoclonal IgG bands on electrophoresis or isoelectric focusing—indicating restricted clonal expansion of antibody-producing cells within the CNS—is a hallmark finding, seen in over 85% of confirmed cases. Unlike infectious or inflammatory conditions, CSF glucose is normal, and protein levels may be mildly elevated. The 14-3-3 protein is associated with Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease, not MS, and decreased IgG concentration would argue against intrathecal synthesis. Thus, oligoclonal bands remain a key diagnostic marker for MS.

question:
A 22-year-old primigravida presents for a regular prenatal visit at 16 weeks gestation. She is concerned about the results of a dipstick test she performed at home, which showed 1+ glucose. She does not know  if her liquid consumption has increased, but she urinates more frequently than before. The course of her pregnancy has been unremarkable and she has no significant co-morbidities. The BMI is 25.6 kg/cm2 and she has gained 3 kg (6.72 lb) during the pregnancy. The blood pressure is 110/80 mm Hg, the heart rate is 82/min, the respiratory rate is 14/min, and the temperature is 36.6℃ (97.9℉). The lungs are clear to auscultation, the heart sounds are normal with no murmurs, and there is no abdominal or costovertebral angle tenderness. The laboratory tests show the following results:
Fasting glucose 97 mg/L
ALT 12 IU/L
AST 14 IU/L
Total bilirubin 0.8 mg/dL(15 µmol/L)
Plasma creatinine 0.7 mg/dL (61.9 µmol/L)
Which of the following tests are indicated to determine the cause of the abnormal dipstick test results?
passage: During pregnancy, physiological changes in renal function often lead to a lowered renal threshold for glucose, resulting in glucosuria even when blood glucose levels are normal. This occurs because the glomerular filtration rate increases significantly, while tubular reabsorption of glucose does not keep pace, causing glucose to appear in the urine. Such glucosuria is particularly common in the second trimester and is typically benign, resolving after delivery. In a healthy pregnant woman with no risk factors for diabetes, a single dipstick finding of trace to 1+ glucose does not warrant further investigation, especially when fasting blood glucose is within normal range and there are no other concerning symptoms, such as polyuria (which can be a normal pregnancy symptom) or excessive weight gain. Additional testing, such as an oral glucose tolerance test or HbA1c measurement, is reserved for women with risk factors like obesity, prior gestational diabetes, or elevated fasting glucose levels. Routine urinalysis may be performed as part of prenatal care but is not specifically indicated for isolated, mild glucosuria in the absence of proteinuria, infection, or other abnormalities. Thus, in low-risk pregnancies, no further tests are required for incidental glucosuria.

question: A 9-month-old boy is brought to the physician by his mother because of intermittent watery diarrhea for several months. Pregnancy and delivery were uncomplicated. He was diagnosed with eczematous dermatitis at 3 months old. His height and weight are below the 5th percentile. Immunologic evaluation shows a defect in activated regulatory T cells. A genetic analysis shows a mutation in the FOXP3 gene. This patient is at increased risk for which of the following?
passage: IPEX syndrome (Immune dysregulation, Polyendocrinopathy, Enteropathy, X-linked) is a severe autoimmune disorder caused by mutations in the FOXP3 gene, which encodes a transcription factor critical for the development and function of regulatory T cells (Tregs). The loss of functional Tregs leads to unchecked activation of effector T cells, resulting in multiorgan autoimmunity. Clinical onset typically occurs in early infancy, with intractable watery diarrhea, eczematous dermatitis, and failure to thrive. Affected infants are at high risk for autoimmune endocrinopathies, most commonly type 1 diabetes mellitus and thyroiditis, which may present in the first year of life. Other features can include autoimmune cytopenias, hepatitis, and nephritis. Laboratory findings often reveal elevated IgE levels, eosinophilia, and circulating autoantibodies. Diagnosis is confirmed by genetic testing demonstrating a FOXP3 mutation. Without treatment, the condition is frequently fatal; management includes immunosuppression and, eventually, hematopoietic stem cell transplantation. The triad of enteropathy, eczema, and endocrinopathy in a male infant is highly suggestive of IPEX syndrome.

question: A 57-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 3-week history of abdominal bloating and increased frequency of stools. He describes the stools as bulky, foul-smelling, and difficult to flush. He also has a 4-month history of recurrent dull upper abdominal pain that usually lasts for a few days, worsens after meals, and is not relieved with antacids. He has had a 10-kg (22-lb) weight loss in the past 4 months. He has no personal or family history of serious illness. He has smoked 1 pack of cigarettes daily for 37 years. He has a 12-year history of drinking 6 to 8 beers daily. He is 160 cm (5 ft 3 in) tall and weighs 52 kg (115 lb); BMI is 20 kg/m2. His vital signs are within normal limits. Abdominal examination shows mild epigastric tenderness without rebound or guarding. Bowel sounds are normal. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?
passage: Chronic pancreatitis is a progressive inflammatory condition of the pancreas characterized by irreversible structural damage and exocrine insufficiency. Classic clinical features include steatorrhea—bulky, foul-smelling stools that are difficult to flush—due to malabsorption of fats, along with epigastric or upper abdominal pain that typically worsens after meals and is not relieved by antacids. Weight loss is common, often resulting from both malabsorption and reduced caloric intake. Major risk factors include prolonged heavy alcohol consumption and cigarette smoking, both of which contribute to pancreatic injury. Diagnosis begins with a thorough history and physical examination, followed by imaging to evaluate pancreatic morphology. Abdominal computed tomography (CT) is the initial imaging modality of choice, as it can detect pancreatic calcifications, ductal dilation, atrophy, and masses with high sensitivity. CT is preferred over ultrasound because of its superior resolution and ability to visualize the pancreas despite overlying bowel gas or obesity, and it is more appropriate than upper endoscopy for assessing structural pancreatic disease.

question: A 67-year-old white man presents to his primary care provider for an annual examination. He notes a pink bump that has rapidly developed on his forehead over the last month. In the past, he has had significant sun exposure including multiple blistering sunburns. The physical examination reveals a 2 cm dome-shaped plaque with a central keratin plug (as shown in the image). Excisional biopsy of the lesion reveals an exophytic nodule with central invagination, full of keratin. Keratinocyte atypia is minimal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
passage: Keratoacanthoma is a rapidly growing, benign epithelial tumor that typically arises on sun-exposed skin of fair-skinned individuals, particularly older adults with a history of significant ultraviolet exposure and blistering sunburns. Clinically, it presents as a firm, dome-shaped plaque with a characteristic central keratin plug, often reaching 1–2 cm in diameter within weeks. Histopathologically, the lesion appears as an exophytic, cup-shaped invagination filled with laminated keratin, lined by well-differentiated squamous epithelium showing minimal cellular atypia. The abrupt onset and distinctive architecture help differentiate it from squamous cell carcinoma, which exhibits more pronounced cytologic atypia and invasive growth. Despite its benign nature, keratoacanthoma may occasionally regress spontaneously, but because of its rapid expansion and resemblance to malignancy, excisional biopsy is generally performed for both diagnosis and treatment.

question: A 56-year-old man presents to his primary care doctor with intermittent chest pain. He reports a 2-month history of exertional chest pain that commonly arises after walking 5 or more blocks. He describes the pain as dull, burning, non-radiating substernal pain. His past medical history is notable for hypercholesterolemia and hypertension. He takes simvastatin and losartan. His temperature is 98.9°F (37.2°C), blood pressure is 150/85 mmHg, pulse is 88/min, and respirations are 18/min. On exam, he is well-appearing and in no acute distress. S1 and S2 are normal. No murmurs are noted. An exercise stress test is performed to further evaluate the patient’s pain. Which of the following substances is released locally to increase coronary blood flow during exertion?
passage: During exercise, the heart's increased metabolic demand requires a proportional rise in coronary blood flow to deliver oxygen and nutrients. This local vasodilation is primarily mediated by metabolic autoregulation, where substances released from cardiac myocytes in response to hypoxia or heightened metabolism act directly on coronary arterioles. Adenosine is the most prominent of these local mediators. Produced from the breakdown of adenosine triphosphate (ATP) during periods of increased work or reduced oxygen availability, adenosine diffuses to vascular smooth muscle and binds to A2 receptors, activating a signaling cascade that leads to vasodilation. This mechanism ensures that coronary flow closely matches myocardial oxygen consumption. Other factors, such as nitric oxide and prostacyclin, contribute to basal tone and shear-stress responses, but adenosine is the key signal for rapid, demand-driven increases in flow. Pathological conditions like coronary artery stenosis can blunt this response, contributing to exertional ischemia and symptoms such as chest pain. Understanding this regulatory process is essential for interpreting exercise stress testing, where inadequate flow augmentation may indicate significant obstructive disease.

question:
A 69-year-old male with a history of metastatic small cell lung carcinoma on chemotherapy presents to his oncologist for a follow-up visit. He has responded well to etoposide and cisplatin with plans to undergo radiation therapy. However, he reports that he recently developed multiple “spots” all over his body. He denies any overt bleeding from his gums or joint swelling. His past medical history is notable for iron deficiency anemia, osteoarthritis, and paraneoplastic Lambert-Eaton syndrome. He has a 40 pack-year smoking history. His temperature is 98.5°F (36.9°C), blood pressure is 130/70 mmHg, pulse is 115/min, and respirations are 20/min. On examination, a rash is noted diffusely across the patient’s trunk and bilateral upper and lower extremities.

Results from a complete blood count are shown below:
Hemoglobin: 11.9 mg/dl
Hematocrit: 35%
Leukocyte count: 5,000/mm^3
Platelet count: 20,000/mm^3

The oncologist would like to continue chemotherapy but is concerned that the above results will limit the optimal dose and frequency of the regimen. A recombinant version of which of the following is most appropriate in this patient?
passage: Chemotherapy-induced thrombocytopenia is a common complication of myelosuppressive regimens, particularly those used to treat malignancies such as small cell lung carcinoma. When platelet counts fall below safe thresholds (e.g., 20,000/mm³), the risk of spontaneous bleeding increases, and dose reductions or treatment delays may become necessary. Recombinant interleukin-11 (IL-11, oprelvekin) is a thrombopoietic growth factor that stimulates megakaryocyte maturation and platelet production. It is approved for the prevention of severe thrombocytopenia in patients undergoing nonmyeloablative chemotherapy who are at high risk for bleeding. By promoting platelet recovery, IL-11 enables the continuation of optimal chemotherapy dosing and scheduling. In contrast, granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF) targets neutrophil recovery, while interleukins such as IL-2 and IL-8 have different immune-modulating or chemotactic roles and are not used to raise platelet counts. Therefore, in patients with significant chemotherapy-induced thrombocytopenia, recombinant IL-11 is the most appropriate agent to support platelet production and minimize treatment interruptions.

question: A 60-year-old man with a history of osteoarthritis has been awaiting hip replacement surgery for 3 years. During his annual physical, he reports that he has been taking over the counter pain medications, but that no amount of analgesics can relieve his constant pain. Laboratory results reveal that his renal function has deteriorated when compared to his last office visit 2 years ago. Serum creatinine is 2.0 mg/dL, and urinalysis shows 1+ proteinuria. There are no abnormalities seen on microscopy of the urine. A renal biopsy shows eosinophilic infiltration and diffuse parenchymal inflammation. What is the most likely explanation for this patient's deterioration in renal function?
passage: Analgesic nephropathy, a form of toxic tubulointerstitial nephritis, is a common cause of chronic kidney disease in patients who consume large quantities of over-the-counter pain relievers, particularly nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) or combination analgesics. This condition results from direct tubular toxicity and cell-mediated immune reactions, leading to diffuse parenchymal inflammation and eosinophilic infiltration on renal biopsy. Clinically, patients often present with slowly progressive renal failure, sterile pyuria, and low-grade proteinuria without significant hematuria or cellular casts. Serum creatinine rises gradually, and the renal function decline may be insidious. Unlike acute tubular necrosis, which typically shows muddy brown granular casts and is associated with ischemia or direct tubular toxins, toxic tubulointerstitial nephritis prominently involves an inflammatory interstitial response. Long-term use of analgesics, especially in older adults with underlying osteoarthritis, increases the risk. Discontinuation of the offending agent can halt progression but rarely reverses established fibrosis.

question: A 62-year-old man presents to the emergency department with shortness of breath. The patient says he feels as if he is unable to take a deep breath. The patient has a past medical history of COPD and a 44-pack-year smoking history. The patient has been admitted before for a similar presentation. His temperature is 98.7°F (37.1°C), blood pressure is 177/118 mmHg, pulse is 123/min, respirations are 33/min, and oxygen saturation is 80% on room air. The patient is started on 100% oxygen, albuterol, ipratropium, magnesium, and prednisone. The patient claims he is still short of breath. Physical exam reveals bilateral wheezes and poor air movement. His oxygen saturation is 80%. Which of the following is the best next step in management?
passage: Noninvasive positive pressure ventilation (NIPPV), most commonly administered as bilevel positive airway pressure (BiPAP), is a cornerstone in the management of acute exacerbations of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) accompanied by respiratory failure. Patients presenting with dyspnea, tachypnea, accessory muscle use, hypoxia, and hypercapnia—despite initial bronchodilator and corticosteroid therapy—are appropriate candidates for NIPPV. BiPAP provides inspiratory positive airway pressure to augment tidal volume and expiratory positive airway pressure to counteract dynamic airway collapse, thereby reducing work of breathing, improving gas exchange, and decreasing the need for invasive mechanical ventilation. Indications for BiPAP include moderate to severe respiratory distress with pH < 7.35 and PaCO₂ > 45 mmHg, or persistent hypoxemia despite supplemental oxygen. Contraindications include respiratory arrest, severe encephalopathy, hemodynamic instability, facial trauma, or inability to protect the airway. Early initiation of NIPPV in appropriate COPD exacerbations has been shown to lower intubation rates, shorten hospital stays, and reduce mortality. Intubation is reserved for patients who fail or cannot tolerate NIPPV, or those with worsening respiratory acidosis, altered mental status, or hemodynamic compromise.

question: A 76-year-old man is brought to the physician by his wife because of low back pain and weakness for the past 4 weeks. He says that the pain is sharp, radiates to his left side, and has a burning quality. He has had a cough occasionally productive of blood-streaked sputum for the past 2 months. He has had 3.2-kg (7.0-lb) weight loss in that time. He is now unable to walk without assistance and has had constipation and difficulty urinating for the past 2 weeks. He has hypertension treated with enalapril. He has smoked 1 pack of cigarettes daily for 60 years. His temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 75/min, and blood pressure is 150/80 mm Hg. He is oriented to person, place, and time. Neurologic examination shows 3/5 strength of the lower extremities. Deep tendon reflexes are hyperreflexive. Babinski sign is present bilaterally. Sensory examination shows decreased sensation to pinprick below the T4 dermatome. He is unable to lie recumbent due to severe pain. An x-ray of the chest shows multiple round opacities of varying sizes in both lungs. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?
passage: Metastatic spinal cord compression (MSCC) is an oncologic emergency that occurs when a tumor, most commonly from lung, breast, or prostate cancer, invades the epidural space and compresses the spinal cord. Patients typically present with progressive, localized back pain that may radiate and have a burning quality, followed by motor weakness, sensory loss below a distinct dermatomal level (often assessed by pinprick), hyperreflexia, Babinski sign, and autonomic dysfunction such as urinary retention and constipation. Prompt recognition is critical because neurological deficits can become irreversible. The initial management involves administration of high-dose corticosteroids, such as intravenous dexamethasone, to reduce peritumoral edema and inflammation, thereby preserving neurologic function while definitive treatment is arranged. Definitive therapy usually includes radiation therapy or, in selected cases, surgical decompression. Other interventions, such as antibiotics or antivirals, are not indicated unless an infectious cause is suspected. In patients with a history of smoking, hemoptysis, weight loss, and multiple round opacities on chest imaging, lung cancer is the likely primary etiology. The combination of these findings with neurological deficits below T4 warrants immediate corticosteroids to prevent further deterioration.

question: A 32-year-old man arrives to his primary care physician to discuss his fear of flying. The patient reports that he has had a fear of flying since being a teenager. He went on a family vacation 15 years ago, and during the flight there was turbulence and a “rough landing”. Since then he has avoided flying. He did not go to his cousin’s wedding because it was out of the country. He also was unable to visit his grandmother for her 80th birthday. The last time his job asked him to meet a client out of state, he drove 18 hours instead of flying. Two years ago he promised his fiancé they could fly to Florida.
