Otherwise he had no neurologic deficits prior to 6 weeks ago. Physical exam reveals myoclonus and ataxia. Which of the following is true of the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

options.A: Can be transmitted on medical equipment even after autoclaving instruments
options.B: Contains genetic material but is unable to survive independent from a host
options.C: Has a cell wall containing peptidoglycan
options.D: Leads to intracellular aggregations of hyperphosphorylated tau protein

passage: Prion diseases, also known as transmissible spongiform encephalopathies, are a group of fatal neurodegenerative disorders caused by misfolded prion proteins. Unlike conventional pathogens, prions are infectious proteins that contain no genetic material—neither DNA nor RNA—and consist solely of misfolded conformations of a normal cellular protein. These abnormal prions propagate by inducing the host’s normal prion protein to adopt the same misfolded shape, leading to neuronal damage, spongiform change, and characteristic clinical features. The most common human form is sporadic Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease, which typically presents with rapidly progressive dementia, myoclonus, ataxia, and visual or psychiatric disturbances, often evolving over weeks to months. Prions exhibit extraordinary resistance to conventional sterilization methods, including autoclaving (standard steam sterilization at 121°C) and chemical disinfectants. Special protocols, such as prolonged autoclaving at higher temperatures or treatment with sodium hydroxide, are required to inactivate prions on surgical instruments and medical equipment. This persistence creates a risk of iatrogenic transmission through contaminated neurosurgical instruments, corneal grafts, or dura mater grafts. The absence of a nucleic acid genome and the extreme stability of the prion protein under harsh conditions distinguish it from viruses, bacteria, and other pathogens.

question: A 5-year-old boy is brought to his pediatrician due to sore throat and fever. He was in his usual state of health until 3 days ago when he began to complain of a sore throat as well as general malaise. The patient's past medical history reveals recurrent upper respiratory infections but no skin or soft tissue infections. The child had cardiac surgery as an infant but has otherwise been healthy. On presentation, his temperature is 100°F (37.8 °C), blood pressure is 115/72 mmHg, pulse is 65/min, and respirations are 22/min. Physical exam shows white plaques on the tongue and mouth. A scraping of this material reveals a characteristic morphology after being treated with KOH. Serum protein electrophoresis shows a normal distribution of bands from this patient. Which of the following findings would most likely be found in this patient?

options.A: Delayed separation of the umbilical cord

passage: DiGeorge syndrome, also known as 22q11.2 deletion syndrome, is a primary immunodeficiency disorder caused by a microdeletion on chromosome 22. It results from abnormal development of the third and fourth pharyngeal pouches, leading to thymic hypoplasia or aplasia, parathyroid hypoplasia, and cardiac outflow tract anomalies. The classic clinical triad includes congenital heart disease (often conotruncal defects such as tetralogy of Fallot or interrupted aortic arch), hypoparathyroidism manifesting as hypocalcemia, and T-cell lymphopenia with increased susceptibility to infections. Affected individuals frequently present with recurrent respiratory infections, chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis (oral thrush), and other opportunistic infections. Hypoparathyroidism results in low serum calcium and elevated phosphate levels, which can cause neuromuscular irritability. Cardiac surgery in infancy is common due to structural heart defects. Serum protein electrophoresis is typically normal in DiGeorge syndrome, distinguishing it from other humoral immunodeficiencies. The condition may also include characteristic facies, palatal abnormalities, and autoimmune phenomena, but hypoparathyroidism is a frequent and diagnostically important finding.

question: A 35-year-old woman comes to the physician for sleeping problems and the inability to concentrate for 3 months. She says she is worried because she and her husband have been trying to conceive for more than a year with no success. Conception by in vitro fertilization was attempted once 3 months ago but was unsuccessful. Analysis of her husband's semen has shown normal sperm counts and morphology. She has a 6-year-old daughter who was born at term after an uncomplicated pregnancy. She has no history of severe illness and tries to stay healthy by going to the gym an hour per day. Her menses occur at regular 28-day intervals and last 5 to 6 days; her last menstrual period started 2 days ago. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's inability to conceive?
passage: Diminished ovarian reserve refers to a reduction in the quantity or quality of oocytes remaining in the ovaries, often occurring as a natural consequence of aging. In women over 35, the ovarian follicle pool declines more rapidly, leading to decreased fecundity even when menstrual cycles remain regular and ovulatory. Unlike premature ovarian failure, which involves cessation of menses before age 40, diminished ovarian reserve does not necessarily disrupt cycle regularity. Women with this condition may have normal physical examinations and unremarkable hormonal profiles early in the cycle, but they typically exhibit elevated day-3 follicle-stimulating hormone levels or low anti-Müllerian hormone concentrations. Infertility in such cases often manifests as difficulty conceiving despite regular ovulation, and in vitro fertilization may fail due to poor oocyte yield or quality. Other potential causes of secondary infertility, such as pelvic inflammatory disease or hypogonadotropic hypogonadism, are less likely in the presence of regular menses, a prior successful pregnancy, and a normal physical exam. Diminished ovarian reserve is thus the most plausible explanation for a woman aged 35 presenting with over one year of unsuccessful attempts, a history of a term delivery, and a failed IVF cycle, with no identifiable anatomic or hormonal abnormalities.

question: A 25-year-old female is brought to the emergency department by her family. They found her somnolent next to her computer and scattered pill bottles over two hours ago. The family cannot recall the types of pill bottles that they found and did not bring them to the ED. The patient's past medical history is notable for anxiety, obesity, hypertension, and polycystic ovarian syndrome. She is currently not on any medications other than an exercise program with which she is non-compliant. A physical exam is performed and is within normal limits. The patient begins communicating with the physician and states that she did take acetaminophen but it was only a few pills. Her temperature is 99.5°F (37.5°C), pulse is 82/min, blood pressure is 125/85 mmHg, respirations are 11/min, and oxygen saturation is 97% on room air. Which of the following is the next best step in management?
passage: Acetaminophen (paracetamol) overdose is a leading cause of acute liver failure and requires prompt intervention. The primary toxic metabolite, N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone imine (NAPQI), is normally detoxified by glutathione. In overdose, glutathione stores become depleted, allowing NAPQI to accumulate and cause hepatocellular necrosis. The antidote, N-acetylcysteine (NAC), replenishes glutathione and directly binds NAPQI, preventing liver injury. NAC is most effective when administered within 8 to 10 hours of ingestion, but benefit may still occur beyond that window, particularly in cases of massive overdose or delayed presentation. Activated charcoal may be considered if the patient presents within one to two hours of ingestion, but it is not a substitute for NAC. Syrup of ipecac is no longer recommended due to its unpredictable efficacy and risk of aspiration. Because the reported number of pills is often unreliable—especially in patients with altered mental status, suicidal ideation, or polysubstance ingestion—treatment should not be withheld based on history alone. In any suspected acetaminophen overdose with potential for toxicity, the next best step is immediate administration of N-acetylcysteine, often started empirically while awaiting serum acetaminophen levels. Supportive care, including intravenous fluids, may be provided but does not address the underlying toxicity.

question: A 27-year-old man and his wife come to the physician for preconception genetic counseling. The patient and his wife are asymptomatic and have no history of serious illness. The patient's older brother died at age 13 from heart failure and his 6-year-old nephew currently has severe muscle weakness. Genetic testing in the patient's family shows that his mother is not a carrier of the mutation that is responsible for the disease in his relatives. A pedigree chart of the patient's family is shown. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for the patient not being affected?
passage: Gonadal mosaicism occurs when a genetic mutation arises postzygotically in the germline cells of an individual, such that the mutation is present in a subset of gametes but not in the somatic cells of that individual. As a result, the individual is clinically unaffected and will not test positive for the mutation in a standard blood or tissue sample, yet they can transmit the mutation to their offspring. This phenomenon explains why a parent may have multiple children with an inherited disorder while being asymptomatic and showing no evidence of the mutation in somatic DNA. In contrast to incomplete penetrance, where a mutation is present in all cells but not expressed, gonadal mosaicism involves mutation restriction to the germline. The risk of recurrence in future pregnancies depends on the proportion of affected gametes. This mechanism is particularly relevant in severe dominant disorders, where a seemingly sporadic case in a family can be traced to a parent with gonadal mosaicism, and unaffected siblings may have inherited normal alleles.

question: A 58-year-old Caucasian female presents to her primary care physician with concerns about several recent health changes. The patient has recently gained weight and can't seem to exercise like she used to. She also notes that her thinking seems slow. Physical examination shows thickened, dry skin. Serum analysis confirms the physician's suspicions. Which of the following findings was most helpful in the diagnosis?
passage: Primary hypothyroidism is a common endocrine disorder, particularly in middle-aged and older women, characterized by insufficient production of thyroid hormones from the thyroid gland. The condition typically presents with a constellation of nonspecific symptoms, including weight gain, fatigue, exercise intolerance, cognitive slowing (often described as "brain fog"), and dermatological changes such as thickened, dry skin. These manifestations result from the systemic slowing of metabolic processes due to low circulating levels of thyroxine (T4) and triiodothyronine (T3). In response to diminished thyroid hormone levels, the pituitary gland increases its secretion of thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) via a negative feedback loop. Therefore, the most sensitive and specific laboratory finding in primary hypothyroidism is a markedly elevated serum TSH level. While low T4 and T3 are also present, they can be less reliable due to variations in binding proteins and nonthyroidal illness. Additional findings may include hypercholesterolemia due to reduced lipid metabolism, but this is not diagnostic. An elevated TSH is the cornerstone of confirming the diagnosis of primary hypothyroidism.

question: A 33-year-old woman at 17-weeks gestation comes to the physician for evaluation of a painless mass in the right breast for the last 3 weeks that has not increased in size. Physical examination shows dense breasts and a 2.5-cm rubbery, mobile mass that is nontender in the upper outer quadrant of the right breast. A biopsy of the mass shows rounded contour with overgrowth of fibrous and glandular tissue. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
passage: A fibroadenoma is a benign breast tumor composed of both fibrous and glandular tissue, commonly presenting as a painless, rubbery, mobile mass, often located in the upper outer quadrant. These tumors typically feel smooth and well-circumscribed, and they are most frequently diagnosed in women of reproductive age. Histologically, fibroadenomas exhibit a rounded contour with proliferation of fibrous stroma and glandular elements, often showing a pericanalicular or intracanalicular growth pattern. During pregnancy, hormonal changes—particularly elevated estrogen and progesterone—can sometimes stimulate enlargement of fibroadenomas, but existing lesions may remain stable in size. The clinical presentation of a solitary, nontender, freely mobile breast mass with the described histological features is characteristic of a fibroadenoma. Dense breast tissue on examination does not preclude this diagnosis, as fibroadenomas can be palpated even in dense parenchyma. While other breast masses—such as phyllodes tumors or areas of fat necrosis—can occasionally mimic fibroadenomas, the typical histologic appearance of a benign, encapsulated fibroepithelial lesion confirms the diagnosis. Management often includes observation or excision if the mass grows or causes symptoms.

question: A 27-year-old male presents for emergency surgery to reattach his left arm, severed during a high-speed car accident, and has lost significant amounts of blood. His wife arrives within minutes to the hospital exclaiming that under no circumstance should he receive any blood transfusions during surgery. After reviewing his medical chart, you see also see an annotation indicating no blood transfusions for religious reasons. Knowing that he needs a blood transfusion to save his life, what do you do?

options.A: Administer a blood transfusion to save the patient's life
options.B: Call the ethics board of the hospital to discuss the issue
options.C: Respect the patient's written instructions and do not adminster a blood transfusion
options.D: Convince the wife to administer a blood transfusion to her husband

passage: In medical ethics and law, the principle of patient autonomy holds that a competent adult has the right to refuse medical treatment, even if that refusal leads to death. This right extends to life-saving interventions such as blood transfusions. When a patient has provided clear, written advance directives—such as a living will or a signed refusal of blood products based on religious beliefs—healthcare providers are legally and ethically bound to honor those instructions, provided the patient was competent at the time the directive was made. The doctrine of informed consent requires that no treatment be given without voluntary authorization from the patient or a legally authorized surrogate. In emergency situations, if a patient’s documented wishes are known, they override the presumption of implied consent for life-saving care. The patient’s family members, including spouses, may express their own views, but only the patient’s prior competent directive holds legal authority. Therefore, when a medical chart explicitly notes a refusal of blood transfusions for religious reasons, and the patient is unable to speak for himself, the medical team must respect that written directive, even if other alternatives—such as contacting an ethics committee or attempts to convince the spouse—might be considered. The core obligation remains to follow the patient’s expressed autonomous choice.

question: The parents of a 14-year-old patient are concerned and have questions about the use of insulin for their son’s recently diagnosed type 1 diabetes. The patient has developed an upper respiratory infection while at school. He is coughing and has a runny nose. His temperature is 37.8° C (100.2° F) and vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination is unremarkable. Which of the following modifications to his insulin regimen would you recommend to this patient and his parents?

options.A: Increase the frequency of blood glucose checks.
options.D: Hold insulin until the patient gets better.

passage: In children with type 1 diabetes, intercurrent illnesses such as upper respiratory infections can significantly affect blood glucose control. Stress from infection triggers the release of counterregulatory hormones (e.g., cortisol, epinephrine), which promote hyperglycemia and increase insulin resistance. Even if the child is unable to eat normally, the risk of diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) rises because insulin deficiency can develop rapidly. Standard sick-day management guidelines emphasize that insulin should never be withheld or reduced solely due to illness; doing so can precipitate DKA. Instead, patients are advised to monitor blood glucose more frequently—typically every 2–4 hours—to guide insulin adjustments. Additional measures may include checking urine or blood ketones and maintaining hydration with sugar-free or carbohydrate-containing fluids as needed. The goal is to maintain basal insulin coverage and treat hyperglycemia aggressively while avoiding hypoglycemia from reduced food intake. Thus, during a mild febrile illness with stable vital signs, the appropriate modification is to increase the frequency of blood glucose checks rather than altering the insulin dose prematurely.

question: A researcher evaluates healthy breast tissue from 100 women, 50 women that were pregnant at the time of the study and 50 age-matched non-pregnant women. The breast tissue in pregnant women contained an increased number of acinar glands with epithelial proliferation compared to the non-pregnant women. Which process caused this change?
passage: During pregnancy, the mammary glands undergo significant structural and functional changes in preparation for lactation. These changes are primarily driven by hormonal signals, including elevated levels of estrogen, progesterone, and prolactin. One key adaptation is the proliferation of epithelial cells lining the acinar glands, leading to an increase in the number of these glandular units. This process, termed hyperplasia, results in a greater quantity of epithelial cells within the breast tissue, rather than an enlargement of individual cells (hypertrophy). The hyperplastic response ensures that sufficient milk-producing structures are available after parturition. In contrast, non-pregnant women maintain a baseline state without such proliferative activity. The observed increase in acinar glands and epithelial proliferation in pregnant women compared to age-matched non-pregnant controls is thus a classic example of hyperplasia, a normal physiological adaptation to meet the demands of lactation. This change is reversible after weaning, with involution of the glandular tissue.

question: A 21-month-old boy is brought to the physician for a well-child examination. His mother noticed deformities in both of his legs since he started walking independently. He has been healthy apart from an upper respiratory tract infection 6 months ago. He was delivered at 38 weeks' gestation. His 6-year-old sister was treated for developmental dysplasia of the hip. He can kick a ball and say a 2-word phrase. He plays well with other children at his day care. His immunizations are up-to-date. He is at the 40th percentile for height and 50th percentile for weight. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows closed anterior and posterior fontanelles. The knees do not stay together when both the feet and ankles are placed together. The gait is unremarkable. The mother is concerned that he has a growth disorder. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
passage: In early childhood, the development of lower limb alignment follows a predictable pattern. Infants are typically born with varus alignment (bowing) of the legs, which gradually corrects to a neutral or valgus (knock-kneed) alignment by around 18–24 months. This physiologic genu varum is a normal variant, especially common in toddlers during the early stages of independent walking. It is distinguished from pathologic conditions such as rickets or Blount disease by the absence of other concerning features: normal growth parameters, closed fontanelles at appropriate ages, normal vitamin D status and dietary history, and symmetric, non-progressive bowing that resolves spontaneously. Radiographic evaluation is not indicated unless the bowing persists beyond age 2–3 years, is unilateral, rapidly worsening, or associated with short stature, metabolic abnormalities, or gait disturbances. In a healthy, well-nourished child with appropriate developmental milestones and no signs of underlying disease, the most appropriate management is reassurance and continued observation, as the condition typically corrects without intervention. Bracing or vitamin D supplementation is reserved for confirmed pathologic cases.

question: A 30-year-old woman presents to an outpatient clinic for a scheduled gynecologic check-up. The patient appears well and has no specific complaints. She is current with her Pap smears, all of which have been normal. Her menstrual periods are regular and usually last 3–4 days. She denies any recent travel or a history of trauma. The patient has not been sexually active for the past 2 months. She smokes 1 pack of cigarettes per day and drinks alcohol occasionally, but has never used illicit drugs. The pelvic examination reveals a small, firm, non-tender, immobile cystic mass at the edge of the exocervical os with a yellow hue. The patient is otherwise asymptomatic and has no other complaints. The remainder of the physical examination is unremarkable. Which of the following is the next best step in management?
passage: Nabothian cysts, also known as retention cysts of the cervix, are common benign gynecologic findings. They form when squamous epithelium overgrows and obstructs the ducts of endocervical glands, causing mucus to accumulate. Clinically, they appear as small, firm, non-tender, immobile nodules near the external cervical os, often with a yellowish or translucent hue. These cysts are typically asymptomatic and require no treatment. They are frequently discovered incidentally during routine pelvic examination and are considered normal variants of cervical anatomy. No further intervention is indicated, as Nabothian cysts have no malignant potential and do not cause pain, bleeding, or discharge. Management consists of reassurance and continued routine gynecologic surveillance. Differential diagnoses include cervical polyps, endometriosis, or neoplasia, but the characteristic appearance and lack of symptoms usually confirm the benign nature. Biopsy, colposcopy, or hormonal therapy are unnecessary for uncomplicated Nabothian cysts.

question: A 1900-g (4-lb 3-oz) newborn is delivered at term to a 36-year-old primigravid woman. Pregnancy was complicated by polyhydramnios. Apgar scores are 7 and 7 at 1 and 5 minutes, respectively. He is at the 2nd percentile for head circumference and 15th percentile for length. Examination shows a prominent posterior part of the head. The ears are low-set and the jaw is small and retracted. The fists are clenched, with overlapping second and third fingers. The calcaneal bones are prominent and the plantar surface of the foot shows a convex deformity. Abdominal examination shows an omphalocele. Further evaluation of this patient is most likely to show which of the following findings?
passage: Trisomy 18, also known as Edwards syndrome, is a chromosomal disorder caused by the presence of an extra copy of chromosome 18. It is characterized by a distinct constellation of physical anomalies, including intrauterine growth restriction, polyhydramnios, low-set ears, micrognathia (small, retracted jaw), prominent occiput, and rocker-bottom feet with prominent calcanei. Clenched fists with overlapping fingers (typically the second over the third) are classic, as are omphaloceles and other abdominal wall defects. Cardiac malformations are present in over 90% of affected infants; the most common is ventricular septal defect, though other defects such as atrial septal defect or patent ductus arteriosus may also occur. The condition is associated with advanced maternal age and carries a poor prognosis, with most infants surviving only weeks to months. Diagnosis is confirmed by karyotyping, and management is supportive.

question: A 51-year-old man presents to his primary care provider complaining of fatigue, weight loss, and intermittent fevers for the past 6 months. He has had sexual intercourse with multiple men and women over the past year and does not use barrier protection. His last screen for sexually transmitted diseases was 10 years ago and was normal. His past medical history is notable for well-controlled asthma. He was involved in a motorcycle accident 2 years ago and suffered multiple fractured bones in his upper and lower extremities, all of which have healed. He has a distant history of cocaine abuse. He drinks 5-6 glasses of wine per week. His temperature is 100.8°F (38.2°C), blood pressure is 130/75 mmHg, pulse is 85/min, and respirations are 20/min. On examination, he appears cachectic but is pleasant and appropriately interactive. An HIV differentiation assay and follow-up Western blot are performed and are positive. The patient is started on tenofovir, abacavir, and efavirenz. However, the patient develops a hypersensitivity reaction to abacavir, so the decision is made to replace this medication with ritonavir. Which of the following adverse effects is this patient at greatest risk for as a result of this change in medication regimen?
passage: Protease inhibitors (PIs), such as ritonavir, are a cornerstone of antiretroviral therapy for HIV infection, often used in combination with other drug classes like nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) and non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs). Ritonavir is frequently employed as a pharmacokinetic booster due to its potent inhibition of cytochrome P450 3A4, thereby increasing plasma levels of co-administered PIs. However, this class of medications is associated with a distinct metabolic adverse effect profile, most notably lipodystrophy. Lipodystrophy refers to abnormal fat redistribution, characterized by peripheral fat loss (e.g., in the face, arms, and legs) and central fat accumulation (e.g., in the abdomen, dorsocervical region, and breasts). This condition is also linked to insulin resistance and dyslipidemia, increasing cardiovascular risk. In contrast, other antiretroviral agents have different toxicity patterns: NRTIs like abacavir are known for hypersensitivity reactions, tenofovir for renal and bone effects, and efavirenz for central nervous system disturbances. PIs generally do not cause significant hepatotoxicity in standard doses unless combined with certain other drugs, and myelosuppression is not a typical PI-related toxicity. Peripheral neuropathy is more commonly associated with older NRTIs such as didanosine or stavudine. When a PI like ritonavir is introduced into a regimen, careful monitoring for metabolic changes, including lipodystrophy, is warranted.

question:
A 68-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with complaints of increased fatigue and back pain for the past year. His symptoms are progressively getting worse over the past few months despite adequate rest and trial of over the counter analgesics. His back pain is constant and non-radiating in nature. He denies any trauma to his back. His past medical history is insignificant. On examination, there is mild tenderness over the lumbar region in the midline with an absence of paraspinal muscle tenderness. Straight leg raise test is negative. His skin and conjunctiva appear pale. The physician orders basic blood work. His results are provided below.
Complete blood count
RBC 4.9 million cells/µL
Hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL
Hematocrit 41%
Total leukocyte count 6,800 cells/µL
Neutrophils 70%
Lymphocyte 26%
Monocytes 3%
Eosinophil 1%
Basophils 0%
Platelets 230,000 cells/µL
Basic Metabolic Panel
Sodium 136 mEq/L
Potassium 5.1 mEq/L
Chloride 101 mEq/L
Bicarbonate 24 mEq/L
Albumin 3.6 mg/ dL
Urea nitrogen 31 mg/dL
Creatinine 2.7 mg/dL
Uric Acid 6.7 mg/dL
Calcium 12.1 mg/dL
Glucose 105 mg/dL
Urinalysis shows proteinuria without any RBCs or pus cells. The patient’s proteinuria best fits in which of the following category?
passage: Overflow proteinuria occurs when the plasma concentration of low-molecular-weight proteins exceeds the renal tubular reabsorption capacity, leading to their excretion in the urine. This type is distinct from glomerular or tubular proteinuria and is most commonly associated with multiple myeloma, where monoclonal immunoglobulin light chains (Bence Jones proteins) are produced in large quantities. These light chains freely pass through the glomerular filtration barrier but overwhelm the proximal tubule’s ability to reabsorb them. Consequently, they appear in the urine, often without significant albuminuria or accompanying red blood cells or casts. Clinically, overflow proteinuria is suggested by a disproportionate amount of protein in the urine relative to the degree of albuminuria, and it is frequently accompanied by other systemic features such as anemia, bone pain with lytic lesions, hypercalcemia, and renal impairment. Laboratory findings may reveal elevated serum calcium, elevated creatinine, and a normocytic anemia, while urinalysis shows proteinuria with minimal sediment abnormalities. This contrasts with transient proteinuria, which resolves with the inciting stress; orthostatic proteinuria, which occurs only in the upright position; and isolated proteinuria, which is persistent but has no underlying systemic disease.

question: A 68-year-old postmenopausal woman presents to the clinic for a dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) scan. The patient states that she has mild joint pain, which she attributes to osteoarthritis, but she notes no recent fractures. She says that her mother had osteoporosis and she is concerned that she may develop it as well. She takes calcium and vitamin D supplements daily. After review of her DEXA scan, her physician states that she has osteopenia. Which of the following is this patient’s most likely DEXA scan result?
passage: Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) is the standard method for measuring bone mineral density (BMD) and diagnosing osteoporosis. Results are reported as T-scores, which compare the patient’s BMD to that of a healthy young adult reference population. According to the World Health Organization classification, a T-score of -1.0 or higher is considered normal, while a T-score between -1.0 and -2.5 indicates osteopenia (low bone mass). Osteoporosis is defined by a T-score of -2.5 or lower. In postmenopausal women, the T-score is the preferred metric for assessing fracture risk, as it reflects the degree of bone loss relative to peak bone mass. A Z-score, in contrast, compares BMD to age-matched peers and is more useful in premenopausal women or younger adults. For a postmenopausal patient presenting with osteopenia, the T-score would fall within the range of -1.0 to -2.5, such as -1.6, which is a common value indicative of moderately reduced bone density. Standard clinical practice also considers other risk factors, including family history of osteoporosis, age, and calcium and vitamin D intake, to guide management and preventive strategies.

question: An investigator is studying the growth pattern of a particular bacterial strain that caused a respiratory disease outbreak in children in rural Venezuela. The bacteria are found to be able to grow in a specialized culture that contains sheep blood, cystine, and potassium tellurite; the colonies are black and have a shining surface. The isolated bacteria are most likely which of the following?
passage: Corynebacterium diphtheriae is a Gram-positive, non-spore-forming rod that is the causative agent of diphtheria, a severe respiratory disease primarily affecting children in regions with low vaccination coverage. This bacterium exhibits a characteristic growth pattern on specialized selective media, such as cystine-tellurite blood agar. This medium contains sheep blood, cystine, and potassium tellurite; the tellurite inhibits many other respiratory flora while allowing C. diphtheriae to grow. After incubation, colonies appear black or dark gray with a distinctive shiny, metallic surface due to the reduction of tellurite to metallic tellurium within the bacterial cells. The presence of cystine enhances growth and toxin production. Outbreaks of diphtheria, like those reported in rural areas of Venezuela, often occur in underimmunized populations. Identification of black, shiny colonies on this medium, combined with microscopic morphology showing typical palisades or "Chinese letter" arrangements, strongly suggests C. diphtheriae. Confirmatory testing includes toxin production assays, as toxigenic strains are responsible for the severe clinical manifestations. Other respiratory pathogens, such as Legionella, Haemophilus, and Bordetella, do not produce these characteristic colonial features on tellurite-containing media.

question: A 7-month-old boy is brought to the physician because of a 2-month history of fatigue, weakness, and difficulty feeding. He was delivered at term to a 32-year-old woman. He is not able to sit upright on his own. He is at the 75th percentile for height and 25th percentile for weight. His temperature is 37.7°C (99.8°F), blood pressure is 110/68 mm Hg, pulse is 150/min, and respirations are 50/min. His tongue protrudes beyond the teeth. Neck veins are distended. Crackles are heard at both lung bases. Cardiac examination shows an S3 gallop. The liver is palpated 2 cm below the right costal margin. Neurologic examination shows profound weakness in proximal and distal muscles of the upper and lower extremities. He has 2+ reflexes bilaterally. A chest x-ray shows cardiomegaly. Serum glucose is 105 mg/dL. Which of the following is the enzyme most likely to be defective in this patient?
passage: Pompe disease, also known as glycogen storage disease type II, is an autosomal recessive disorder caused by a deficiency of lysosomal acid maltase (acid alpha-glucosidase). This enzyme is responsible for breaking down glycogen into glucose within lysosomes. When deficient, glycogen accumulates abnormally in cardiac and skeletal muscle, as well as in the liver and other tissues. The classic infantile form presents within the first few months of life with progressive hypotonia, profound muscle weakness, feeding difficulties, failure to thrive, and macroglossia (protruding tongue). Cardiomegaly is a hallmark feature, often accompanied by signs of heart failure such as tachypnea, distended neck veins, crackles at lung bases, an S3 gallop, and hepatomegaly. Chest radiography typically reveals an enlarged cardiac silhouette. Deep tendon reflexes are usually preserved or slightly diminished in early stages. Without enzyme replacement therapy, the disease is rapidly fatal due to cardiorespiratory failure. This condition is distinct from other glycogen storage diseases, such as McArdle disease (myophosphorylase deficiency) or Cori disease (debranching enzyme deficiency), which primarily affect muscle or liver function without the characteristic cardiac involvement seen in Pompe disease.

question:
A 4-year-old boy with acute lymphoblastic leukemia is admitted to the hospital to undergo allogeneic bone marrow transplantation. Two weeks after the conditioning regimen is started, he develops a temperature of 38.5°C (101.3°F). Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin 8 g/dL
Leukocyte count 1400/mm3
Segmented neutrophils 20%
Eosinophils 0.5%
Lymphocytes 87%
Monocytes 1%
Platelet count 110,000/mm3
Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy for this patient?"
passage: Allogeneic bone marrow transplantation (BMT) is a curative therapy for various hematologic malignancies, including acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL). Prior to infusion of donor stem cells, recipients undergo a conditioning regimen—a combination of high-dose chemotherapy and sometimes total body irradiation—intended to eradicate malignant cells and suppress the host immune system to prevent graft rejection. A predictable consequence of this myeloablative conditioning is severe pancytopenia, particularly neutropenia (absolute neutrophil count [ANC] < 500/mm³), which markedly increases the risk of febrile neutropenia and life-threatening infections. In the early post-transplant period, while waiting for donor stem cells to engraft and restore hematopoiesis, pharmacologic support with myeloid growth factors can accelerate neutrophil recovery. Granulocyte-macrophage colony-stimulating factor (GM-CSF) is a cytokine that stimulates the proliferation and differentiation of myeloid progenitor cells into neutrophils, monocytes, and macrophages. It is indicated to shorten the duration of severe neutropenia after BMT, thereby reducing the incidence of febrile episodes and antibiotic requirements. Other growth factors such as erythropoietin stimulate red blood cell production and interleukin‑5 primarily stimulates eosinophil production, making them less appropriate for managing neutropenia. Thus, in a patient with febrile neutropenia following conditioning for allogeneic BMT, the administration of GM-CSF is the most suitable pharmacotherapy to support early myeloid engraftment and combat infection risk.

question:
A 27-year-old female is brought to the emergency department by police when she attacked her date outside of a pizza restaurant. The patient refused to explain her behavior to the police. She seemed to be responding to internal stimuli when being initially interviewed. The patient has a past medical history of depression, anxiety, and obesity. The patient has a known history of medication non-compliance. Her temperature is 99.5°F (37.5°C), pulse is 112/min, blood pressure is 100/70 mmHg, respirations are 18/min, and oxygen saturation is 98% on room air. On physical exam the patient is poorly kempt and has an odd body odor. Inspection of her oropharynx reveals multiple ulcers. Cardiac exam is unrevealing with the exception of a friction rub. Since the incident, the patient has calmed down and is currently resting. She is no longer responding to internal stimuli and is more cooperative. Lab values are obtained and are below.

Serum:
Na+: 135 mEq/L
Cl-: 100 mEq/L
K+: 3.9 mEq/L
HCO3-: 24 mEq/L
BUN: 20 mg/dL
Glucose: 70 mg/dL
Creatinine: 1.7 mg/dL
Ca2+: 10.0 mg/dL
Mg2+: 1.5 mEq/L

Hemoglobin: 10 g/dL
Hematocrit: 35%
Reticulocyte count: 4.9%
Platelet count: 275,000/mm^3
Leukocyte count: 5,500/mm^3

Which of the following is the next best step in management for this patient's underlying condition?
passage: Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) is a chronic autoimmune disease characterized by multisystem involvement and variable clinical presentations. Common manifestations include constitutional symptoms such as fever and fatigue, mucocutaneous lesions (e.g., oral ulcers), serositis (e.g., pericarditis manifesting as a friction rub), renal involvement (elevated creatinine), and hematologic abnormalities such as hemolytic anemia (suggested by low hemoglobin with elevated reticulocyte count). Neuropsychiatric symptoms, including acute psychosis or altered mental status, can occur due to central nervous system involvement. Diagnosis relies on a combination of clinical and laboratory findings; the presence of antinuclear antibodies (ANA) is nearly universal in SLE, and antibodies to double-stranded DNA (anti-dsDNA) are highly specific for the disease, often correlating with active disease, particularly renal and hematologic involvement. Initial evaluation of suspected SLE includes testing for ANA and anti-dsDNA levels to confirm the diagnosis and guide management. Treatment typically involves immunosuppressive agents tailored to the severity and organ systems affected.

question: A 68-year-old man presents to the emergency department because of fever, abdominal pain, and rapidly progressive distension of the abdomen. His appendix was removed for an abscess 3 days ago. The complete blood count showed leukocytosis. Anaerobic culture of the peritoneal fluid needle aspirate showed a gram-negative pathogen. Which of the following, in addition to polymicrobial infection, is the most likely cause of this patient’s condition?
passage: Intra-abdominal infections, particularly those arising after gastrointestinal surgery or perforation, are typically polymicrobial, involving a mixture of aerobic and anaerobic bacteria. Among the anaerobes, *Bacteroides fragilis* is the most frequently isolated pathogen. This gram-negative, obligate anaerobic rod is a common component of the colonic flora and possesses virulence factors such as a polysaccharide capsule that promotes abscess formation. Postoperative peritonitis, especially following appendectomy for abscess, often results from leakage of bowel contents into the peritoneal cavity. Anaerobic cultures of peritoneal fluid in such settings commonly yield *Bacteroides fragilis*. Its ability to survive in the anaerobic environment of an abscess, combined with its intrinsic resistance to many beta-lactam antibiotics, makes it a key contributor to these infections. Clinical presentation includes fever, abdominal pain, leukocytosis, and progressive abdominal distension. Appropriate antimicrobial therapy must cover both aerobic enteric organisms and anaerobes like *Bacteroides fragilis* to effectively treat the infection.

question: A 31-year-old female patient presents with significant swelling and redness of her fingers that is causing her significant pain. Her right knee has also been stiff and slightly swollen for the past 6 weeks. She is overall fatigued and has difficulty moving around but has not sought medical attention until now. On physical examination, the distal interphalangeal joints are swollen, warm, and painful; her nails are pitted and cracked. There are also rough, erythematous skin patches seen over her elbows which have been present for the last few months. She reports that herer father has a similar condition. They both use hydrating creams and topical glucocorticoids with moderate benefit. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
passage: Psoriatic arthritis is a chronic inflammatory arthritis associated with psoriasis, a skin condition characterized by erythematous, scaly plaques. It typically presents with joint pain, swelling, and stiffness, often affecting the distal interphalangeal (DIP) joints of the fingers. Nail changes, such as pitting, cracking, and onycholysis, are common and help distinguish it from other arthritides. Patients may also experience enthesitis (inflammation at tendon insertion sites) and dactylitis (sausage-like swelling of digits). Systemic symptoms like fatigue are frequent. The condition has a strong genetic component, with family history often present. Skin lesions may precede or coincide with joint symptoms; they typically respond to topical glucocorticoids and emollients, though these do not halt joint disease. Psoriatic arthritis can be confused with other inflammatory arthritides, but the combination of DIP involvement, nail pitting, and psoriatic skin plaques is highly characteristic. Unlike gout, it is not associated with urate crystals; unlike reactive arthritis, it lacks a preceding infection; and unlike dermatomyositis, it does not feature heliotrope rash or Gottron’s papules.

question: A 44-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician because she has been having fever, chest pain, and cough for the last several weeks. She presented to the physician because she discovered blood in her sputum after coughing. She recently returned from a year of traveling abroad, but has otherwise been healthy. Based on clinical suspicion, an acid fast stain is performed showing the causative organism, and she is started on appropriate therapy. Two weeks later, she returns with a skin rash, diarrhea, and confusion, and is given a factor to take in addition to the medications previously prescribed. Which of the following is a characteristic of the factor that was most likely provided?
passage: Isoniazid, a first-line antitubercular agent, can induce pellagra by interfering with the metabolism of tryptophan, a precursor of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NAD). This is because isoniazid competes with pyridoxine (vitamin B6) and inhibits kynureninase, an enzyme critical for the synthesis of niacin from tryptophan. Niacin, also known as vitamin B3, serves as a precursor for the coenzymes NAD and NADP. These coenzymes are essential for numerous oxidation-reduction reactions catalyzed by dehydrogenase enzymes, including those involved in glycolysis, the citric acid cycle, and the electron transport chain. Pellagra is characterized by the classic triad of dermatitis, diarrhea, and dementia, which corresponds to the symptoms of skin rash, diarrhea, and confusion seen in this patient. Treatment involves high-dose niacin supplementation in addition to discontinuing or adjusting the offending medication. Thus, the factor provided—niacin—is required specifically for the function of dehydrogenase enzymes across multiple metabolic pathways.

question: A 51-year-old Asian woman presents to her primary care provider with abdominal pain. On examination, she is found to have rebound tenderness in the lower right quadrant of her abdomen. Further workup suggests ovarian cancer, which is confirmed later in the week to be stage 4 with confirmed distant metastases in the lungs and brain. Which of the following routes describes the most likely lymphatic route for this malignancy to have metastasized to the brain?

options.A: Sentinel node - left lumbar trunk - cisterna chyli - right bronchomediastinal trunk - right lymphatic duct - right subclavian vein - systemic circulation
options.B: Sentinel node - left lumbar trunk - cisterna chyli - thoracic duct - left subclavian vein - systemic circulation
options.C: Sentinel node - right lumbar trunk - cisterna chyli - right bronchomediastinal trunk - right lymphatic duct - right subclavian vein - systemic circulation
options.D: Sentinel node - right lumbar trunk - cisterna chyli - thoracic duct - left subclavian vein - systemic circulation

passage: The lymphatic drainage of the ovaries follows a specific pathway that influences metastatic spread in ovarian cancer. The primary lymphatic vessels from the ovaries ascend along the ovarian blood vessels and drain into the right and left lumbar trunks. The right ovarian lymphatic vessels typically join the right lumbar trunk, while the left ovarian vessels drain into the left lumbar trunk. Both lumbar trunks converge at the cisterna chyli, a dilated lymphatic sac located anterior to the first and second lumbar vertebrae. From the cisterna chyli, lymph flows upward through the thoracic duct, which courses along the vertebral column and ultimately empties into the left subclavian vein at the venous angle. Lymph from the right ovarian region therefore travels via the right lumbar trunk to the cisterna chyli, then through the thoracic duct into the systemic venous circulation. Once in the bloodstream, malignant cells can disseminate to distant organs, including the brain, via arterial circulation. This route accounts for the hematogenous metastasis observed in advanced ovarian cancer, particularly when distant sites such as the lungs and brain are involved.

question: A 49-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 1-week history of diarrhea and abdominal bloating. His stools are bulky, foul-smelling, and difficult to flush. Over the past 6 months, he has had recurrent dull epigastric pain that is triggered by meals and lasts for a few days. He drinks 6 to 8 beers daily. Abdominal examination shows mild epigastric tenderness with no rebound or guarding. A CT scan of the abdomen is shown. The structure indicated by the arrows is most likely lined by which of the following?
passage: Pancreatic pseudocysts are fluid-filled sacs that arise as a complication of acute or chronic pancreatitis, most frequently due to alcohol abuse. They develop when pancreatic enzymes and inflammatory exudates accumulate within a confined space, inciting a fibrotic reaction in surrounding tissues. Unlike true pancreatic cysts, pseudocysts lack an epithelial lining; their walls are composed of granulation tissue and fibrous connective tissue formed in response to the ongoing inflammatory process. This distinction is critical for diagnosis and management. Clinically, patients often present with epigastric pain exacerbated by meals, bloating, and steatorrhea characterized by bulky, foul-smelling stools that are difficult to flush—hallmarks of pancreatic exocrine insufficiency. On CT imaging, pseudocysts appear as well-defined, round or oval, hypodense lesions adjacent to the pancreas. Because they are not lined by epithelium, they do not have a risk of malignant transformation, but they may become infected, rupture, or cause obstruction. Treatment typically involves endoscopic, percutaneous, or surgical drainage when symptomatic.

question: An 80-year-old African American man is brought to the emergency department in a confused state with history of general illness for the past week. He is known to be hypertensive, but noncompliant with medications for the last 20 years. Physical examination reveals dry skin, ecchymoses, and conjunctival pallor. His blood pressure is 180/99 mm Hg, heart rate is 89/min, and respiratory rate is 17/min. The initial laboratory results are remarkable for hemoglobin of 10 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume of 90 μm3, platelet count of 200,000/mm3, blood urea nitrogen of 29 mg/dL, and creatinine of 2.1 mg/dL. Which of the following would be the best initial management for this patient's condition?
passage: In patients with chronic kidney disease (CKD) and hypertension, angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors (ACEIs) are often the preferred initial antihypertensive agents due to their renoprotective effects. These medications reduce intraglomerular pressure by dilating efferent arterioles, thereby slowing the progression of nephropathy, particularly in the setting of proteinuria. ACEIs are also beneficial in attenuating the cardiovascular complications associated with CKD. While African American patients may exhibit a blunted response to ACEI monotherapy due to lower baseline renin activity, these agents remain first-line when CKD is present because of their long-term renal benefits. Thiazide diuretics are generally less effective when the glomerular filtration rate falls below 30 mL/min, and calcium channel blockers, though effective for blood pressure reduction, do not provide the same degree of renoprotection. In the context of elevated creatinine, anemia, and poorly controlled hypertension, initiation of an ACEI addresses both the immediate blood pressure goal and the underlying renal disease.

question:
A 4-month-old boy is brought to the pediatrician by his parents. He presents to the pediatric ward with fever, dyspnea, and cough, which he developed 3 days ago. His mother also reports he had poor weight gain despite a good appetite during the past 2 months as well as frequent stools with an unpleasant smell. He was born at 29 weeks via spontaneous vaginal delivery. He is meeting all of his milestones and is up to date with all vaccines. The child is breastfed exclusively. His blood pressure is 80/50 mm Hg, the heart rate is 109/min, the respiratory rate is 29/min, and the temperature is 39.1°C (102.4°F). The patient’s weight is between the 5th and 10th percentile. His length is between the 50th and 75th percentile. The patient is sluggish and reacts torpidly to examination. His skin is pale and dry with decreased turgor and elasticity. On auscultation, there are diminished vesicular sounds and disseminated moist rales at the bases of both lungs. Heart sounds are normal. The abdomen is distended without palpable masses. The patient’s blood analysis shows the following findings:
Complete blood count 
 Erythrocytes  3.3 x 106/mm3
 Hb  12 g/dL
 Total leukocyte count  17,500/mm3
 Neutrophils
 59%
 Lymphocytes
 32%
 Eosinophils
 3%
 Monocytes
 6%
 Basophils
 0
Platelet count
232,000/mm3
Sputum culture grows Pseudomonas aeruginosa. A sweat test shows chloride concentration of 85 mEq/L (elevated). Which of the following is involved in the pathogenesis of this patient’s symptoms?

options.A: Neutrophil elastase damages lung tissue due to lack of alpha-1-antitrypsin.
options.C: Electron transport chain in mitochondria is disrupted due to lack of glycerol-3-phosphate dehydrogenase.
options.D: Due to mutations in dynein, the ciliary epithelium fails to provide appropriate mucociliary clearance.

passage: Cystic fibrosis is an autosomal recessive disorder caused by mutations in the *CFTR* gene, which encodes the cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator (CFTR) protein. This protein functions as a chloride channel on the apical surface of epithelial cells in the lungs, pancreas, intestines, sweat glands, and other exocrine tissues. Defective or absent CFTR protein impairs chloride ion transport, leading to dehydrated, thickened secretions. In the respiratory tract, this results in impaired mucociliary clearance, chronic airway inflammation, and recurrent infections, often with *Pseudomonas aeruginosa*. In the pancreas, obstructed ducts prevent digestive enzyme secretion, causing malabsorption, steatorrhea (foul-smelling, frequent stools), and poor weight gain despite adequate caloric intake. Elevated sweat chloride concentration (>60 mEq/L) is a diagnostic hallmark. The disease may present in infancy with failure to thrive and recurrent pulmonary infections, and it is often identified via newborn screening or clinical features such as chronic cough, dyspnea, and electrolyte abnormalities. Management focuses on airway clearance, pancreatic enzyme replacement, and addressing complications.

question: A 15-year-old girl is brought to the physician by her mother because of worsening grades over the past year. Since she started high school one year ago, her academic performance has decreased. She also has had difficulty finding friends at the new school. She is afraid that her classmates will make fun of her and think that she is “stupid.” One month ago, when she had to give a presentation, she could not stop wondering how her classmates were going to react if she said something wrong. During the presentation, her heart started racing and she became flushed. Since this event, she avoids saying anything in class. She spends her breaks in the restroom because she is worried that nobody will talk to her. Physical and neurologic examinations show no abnormalities. On mental status examination, the girl avoids eye contact and appears uncomfortable and anxious. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy for this patient's condition?
passage: Social anxiety disorder, also known as social phobia, is characterized by an intense fear of negative evaluation or humiliation in social or performance situations. Affected individuals often avoid such situations or endure them with marked distress, leading to significant impairment in academic, occupational, or social functioning. Common physical symptoms include tachycardia, flushing, sweating, and trembling. In adolescents, the condition may manifest as declining school performance, social withdrawal, and refusal to participate in class activities. First-line pharmacotherapy for social anxiety disorder in children and adolescents is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI), such as fluoxetine. SSRIs are preferred due to their efficacy, tolerability, and safety profile compared to older antidepressants like monoamine oxidase inhibitors or tricyclic agents. Beta-blockers, such as propranolol, may be used for situational performance anxiety but are not recommended for generalized social anxiety disorder. Cognitive-behavioral therapy is an essential non-pharmacological intervention often combined with medication for optimal outcomes.

question: A 3-year-old boy is brought to the clinic by his parents because he ‘hasn’t been himself lately’ and reportedly gets tired very easily from his swimming classes in comparison to the other kids. He also ‘can’t catch his breath’ at times. The mother also reports that he seems to be slightly shorter than other children his age. His temperature is 36.6°C (97.9°F), blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg, and respiratory rate is 14/min. On auscultation, a localized harsh pansystolic murmur is heard over the left sternal border at the level of the 2nd–3rd intercostal space. The murmur becomes louder when the patient is asked to squat. An echocardiogram is performed. Which of the structures below gives rise to the defective structure that is causing this patient’s symptoms?
passage: The endocardial cushions are embryonic structures derived from the atrioventricular canal that play a critical role in cardiac septation. During the fifth week of development, the dorsal and ventral endocardial cushions fuse to form the septum intermedium, which divides the atrioventricular canal into left and right orifices. They also contribute to the formation of the membranous portion of the interventricular septum, the atrioventricular valves, and the atrial septum. Defective development or fusion of the endocardial cushions can result in various congenital heart defects, including membranous ventricular septal defects (VSDs). In such cases, a left-to-right shunt develops because the membranous septum fails to close. This leads to increased pulmonary blood flow, causing symptoms such as exercise intolerance, dyspnea, and failure to thrive. On auscultation, a harsh pansystolic murmur is typically heard at the left sternal border. Squatting increases systemic vascular resistance, augmenting the left-to-right shunt and thus intensifying the murmur. Early identification and surgical repair are often necessary to prevent pulmonary hypertension and heart failure.

question: A 26-year-old woman developed rapidly progressive septic shock associated with nuchal rigidity, petechiae, and purpura. She tested positive for both Kernig and Brudzinski's signs and shows signs of confusion, dyspnea, and hypotension. The blood culture results confirmed the presence of Neisseria meningitidis, and a complete blood count (CBC) revealed leukocytosis. Which one of the cytokines below takes part in the pathogenesis of septic shock?
passage: Septic shock is a life-threatening condition characterized by a systemic inflammatory response to infection, often triggered by Gram-negative bacteria such as *Neisseria meningitidis*. The pathogenesis involves the release of bacterial components, like lipopolysaccharides, which stimulate immune cells to produce pro-inflammatory cytokines. Among these, tumor necrosis factor-α (TNF-α) is a primary mediator of septic shock. TNF-α promotes vasodilation, increased vascular permeability, and myocardial depression, leading to hypotension and organ failure. It also induces fever, leukocytosis, and the expression of adhesion molecules, contributing to microvascular thrombosis and disseminated intravascular coagulation. Clinical features such as petechiae, purpura, nuchal rigidity, and positive Kernig and Brudzinski signs indicate meningeal involvement, while confusion and dyspnea reflect systemic hypoperfusion and respiratory compromise. The rapid progression of symptoms underscores the central role of TNF-α in amplifying the inflammatory cascade, making it a key target for therapeutic intervention in septic shock.

question: A 35-year-old man comes to the physician because of episodes of difficulty swallowing for the past 3 months. He feels solid food getting stuck in his chest behind the sternum when he eats. Drinking does not cause any difficulty swallowing. He has no coughing or nasal regurgitation. He has no hoarseness or weight loss. He has had heartburn for 2 years with no response to high-dose omeprazole. His past medical history is also significant for asthma and eczema. He takes no medications except for omeprazole. His vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows no abnormal findings. Which of the following best explains these findings?
passage: Eosinophilic esophagitis (EoE) is a chronic, immune-mediated inflammatory condition of the esophagus characterized by dense eosinophilic infiltration. It commonly presents in young to middle-aged adults with intermittent dysphagia for solid foods, often described as food "getting stuck" behind the sternum. Heartburn is a frequent symptom but is typically refractory to high-dose proton pump inhibitors (PPIs), as the underlying pathogenesis involves an allergic response rather than acid reflux. Patients frequently have a personal or family history of atopic disorders such as asthma, eczema, or allergic rhinitis. Unlike achalasia or diffuse esophageal spasm, EoE does not typically cause regurgitation, coughing, or weight loss. Endoscopic findings may include esophageal rings, furrows, or exudates, but the mucosa can appear normal. Diagnosis is confirmed by esophageal biopsy showing ≥15 eosinophils per high-power field. Management includes dietary elimination of common allergens, topical corticosteroids, and sometimes esophageal dilation for strictures. Differentiating EoE from gastroesophageal reflux disease is crucial given the poor response to acid suppression.

question: A 33-year-old man presents to the emergency department with altered mental status. He was at work as a construction worker when his coworkers found him down at the work site. The patient recently underwent anesthesia for an appendectomy several months ago. He also has a past medical history of schizophrenia well controlled with haloperidol and is currently taking phenytoin for epilepsy. He is also currently taking propranolol for anxiety and hyperthyroidism. His temperature is 106°F (41.1°C), blood pressure is 109/62 mmHg, pulse is 170/min, respirations are 23/min, and oxygen saturation is 95% on room air. Physical exam is notable for an altered man with a Glasgow Coma Scale of 10. He has minimal muscle tone and is incoherent when answering questions. The patient is covered in sweat and dirt. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
passage: Exertional heat stroke is a life-threatening condition characterized by a core body temperature exceeding 104°F (40°C) accompanied by central nervous system dysfunction, such as altered mental status, confusion, or coma. It typically occurs in healthy individuals engaged in strenuous physical activity in hot environments, often with high humidity or inadequate hydration. Physiologically, the body’s thermoregulatory mechanisms become overwhelmed, leading to a failure of heat dissipation. Patients commonly present with profound hyperthermia, tachycardia, hypotension, and profuse sweating, although sweating may cease as the condition progresses. Muscle rigidity is notably absent, distinguishing exertional heat stroke from malignant hyperthermia or neuroleptic malignant syndrome. Immediate management involves rapid cooling and supportive care, as delay can cause multi-organ failure, including renal, hepatic, and cardiac damage. Risk factors include exercise in high ambient temperatures, poor acclimatization, dehydration, and certain medications that impair thermoregulation, such as anticholinergics or beta-blockers (e.g., propranolol). Early recognition is critical for survival.

question:
A 67-year-old man is brought to the hospital by his relatives. He complains of early satiety, abdominal discomfort after food intake, and abdominal bloating. These symptoms have been present since the patient was 52, but they were milder at that time so he paid little attention to them since. Tingling and decreased sensation in both lower extremities began a few months ago. His relatives also note that he has become excessively somnolent and forgetful. The patient’s medical history is significant for acute hepatitis B 20 years ago. He smokes a pack of cigarettes per day and drinks alcohol occasionally. His weight is 61 kg (134.5 lb), height is 181 cm (5 ft 11 in), and BMI is 18.6 kg/m2. His vital signs include: blood pressure 110/80 mm Hg, heart rate 89/min, respiratory rate 13/min, and temperature 36.1°C (96.9°F). The patient’s skin is pale, thin, dry, and hypoelastic. Lymph nodes and the thyroid gland are not enlarged. Lung and heart examinations are within normal limits for his age. The patient’s tongue is bright red with atrophic papillae. The abdomen is distended and tender to palpation in the epigastric area. Neurological examination shows symmetrical bilateral distal hypoesthesia for all sensations and decreased muscle strength in both upper and lower extremities. On a mini-mental status examination, the patient scores 25. The patient’s blood test results are as follows:
Erythrocytes 2.8 x 109/mm3
Hb 8.6 g/dL
Ht 37%
Mean corpuscular hemoglobin  49.9 pg/cell (3.1 fmol/cell)
Mean corpuscular volume 142 µm3 (142 fL)
Reticulocyte count 0.3%
Total leukocyte count 3,070/mm3
Neutrophils 54%
Lymphocyte 32%
Eosinophil 3%
Monocyte 11%
Basophil 0%
Platelet count 195,000/mm3
HbA1C 4.3%
Fasting plasma glucose 4.6 mmol/l (82.9 mg/dL)
Aspartate aminotransferase 22 U/L
Alanine aminotransferase 19 U/L
Amylase 32 U/L
Total bilirubin 32 µmol/L (1.87 mg/dL)
Conjugated bilirubin 8 µmol/L (0.4677 mg/L)
Which of the following options best explains the patient’s neurological and psychiatric symptoms?
passage: Vitamin B12 deficiency, often arising from malabsorption or pernicious anemia, leads to a constellation of hematologic, gastrointestinal, and neurological manifestations. The deficiency impairs the activity of methionine synthase, an enzyme essential for the conversion of homocysteine to methionine and the subsequent synthesis of S-adenosylmethionine (SAMe). SAMe is the primary methyl donor for numerous biochemical reactions, including the methylation of myelin phospholipids, which is critical for maintaining the structural integrity and function of the myelin sheath. Without adequate methylation, myelin becomes unstable, leading to progressive demyelination of peripheral nerves, the spinal cord (particularly the dorsal columns), and the brain. Clinically, this results in symmetric distal paresthesias, loss of vibration and proprioception, muscle weakness, and gait disturbances. Central nervous system involvement can cause cognitive decline, memory loss, somnolence, and psychiatric changes such as depression or psychosis. These neurological and psychiatric symptoms are characteristic of vitamin B12 deficiency and are not explained by chronic bilirubin toxicity, acetaldehyde accumulation, or the influence of pseudo-neurotransmitters, as the underlying mechanism directly involves impaired methylation of myelin phospholipids.

question: A four-day-old neonate is brought to the pediatrician with vaginal discharge for the last two days. Her mother is concerned about the blood-tinged discharge but states that her daughter has been feeding and voiding well. The neonate was delivered at 39 weeks gestation by an uncomplicated vaginal delivery, and she and her mother were discharged home after two days. The prenatal course was complicated by chlamydia in the mother during the first trimester, for which she and the partner were both treated with a negative test of cure. The neonate’s biological father is no longer involved the patient's care, but her mother’s boyfriend has been caring for the baby whenever the mother rests. At this visit, the neonate’s temperature is 98.5°F (36.9°C), pulse is 138/min, and respirations are 51/min. She appears comfortable, and cardiopulmonary and abdominal exams are unremarkable. There are no bruises or marks on her skin. Examination of the genitals reveals no vulvar irritation or skin changes, but there is scant pink mucoid discharge at the introitus. Which of the following is the best next step in management?

options.C: Warm water irrigation of the vagina

passage: In newborn girls, a common and benign condition known as physiologic leukorrhea or pseudomenstruation may occur during the first week of life. This results from the withdrawal of maternal estrogen, which had crossed the placenta and stimulated the neonatal endometrium. The discharge is typically mucoid, scant, and can be clear, white, or blood-tinged. It resolves spontaneously without intervention. When evaluating such a presentation, it is essential to consider infectious causes, such as neonatal vaginitis from maternal sexually transmitted infections (e.g., chlamydia or gonorrhea), but appropriate prenatal treatment and a negative test of cure substantially reduce this risk. Physical examination is unremarkable aside from the discharge, with no signs of irritation, trauma, or abuse. In this context, the neonate is feeding, voiding, and behaving normally. The most appropriate management is reassurance of the caregiver, as no diagnostic or therapeutic procedures are indicated. Unnecessary interventions, such as vaginal cultures or examinations under anesthesia, are not recommended for a self-limited, hormone-mediated process.

question:
A 53-year-old male presents to his primary care physician complaining of fatigue and joint and muscle pain. He reports a 6-month history of slowly progressive tiredness associated with dull achy pain in his knees, hips, and shoulders. His past medical history is notable for hypertension, diabetes, and stable angina. He takes lisinopril, metformin, glyburide, and aspirin. He has a history of intravenous drug use and was homeless in his early 30’s. He drinks alcohol socially and has a 30 pack-year smoking history. His temperature is 98.6°F (37°C), blood pressure is 130/85 mmHg, pulse is 95/min, and respirations are 18/min. Physical examination reveals a generally well-appearing male without evidence of ascites, peripheral edema, or jaundice. Results from laboratory tests are below:

Hepatitis A IgM: Negative
Hepatitis A IgG: Negative
Hepatitis B surface antigen: Negative
Hepatitis B E antigen: Negative
Hepatitis B core antigen: Negative
Hepatitis B surface IgG: Positive
Hepatitis B E IgG: Negative
Hepatitis B core IgG: Negative
Hepatitis C IgG: Positive
HCV RNA: 100,000,000 IU/ml

This patient should be started on which of the following medications?
passage: Chronic hepatitis C virus (HCV) infection is a leading cause of liver disease, often progressing insidiously over years with nonspecific symptoms such as fatigue, arthralgias, and myalgias. Diagnosis is confirmed by the presence of anti-HCV antibodies and detectable HCV RNA in the serum, indicating active viral replication. Without treatment, chronic infection can lead to cirrhosis, hepatic decompensation, and hepatocellular carcinoma. The advent of direct-acting antiviral (DAA) agents has transformed management, offering more than 95% sustained virologic response rates with fewer side effects than older interferon-based regimens. For patients with genotypes 1 or 4, a common initial combination includes sofosbuvir, a nucleotide analog NS5B polymerase inhibitor, plus simeprevir, an NS3/4A protease inhibitor. This oral regimen is highly effective and well-tolerated, even in patients with comorbidities such as hypertension, diabetes, or coronary artery disease. Ribavirin is no longer routinely used with DAAs in non-cirrhotic patients, and lamivudine is a drug for hepatitis B rather than hepatitis C. Interferon-based therapies are now reserved for special cases only. Treatment should be initiated promptly in individuals with viremia to prevent long-term complications of chronic HCV infection.

question: A 40-year-old woman presents to her family physician with a 3-week history of swollen neck. The small, round, and painless swelling, which was detected 3 weeks ago in the middle of her neck, has now increased in size. The swelling is associated with sweaty palms and soles, insomnia, irritability, nervousness, and fatigue; however, the patient does not complain of fever, cervical lymphadenopathy, weight loss, vision problems, or body swelling. The patient’s medical history is negative for similar symptoms or a thyroid disorder. The vital signs are within normal limits. Local examination reveals a 3 x 3 cm round, non-tender, non-fluctuant, and non-pulsatile swelling in the anterior triangle of her neck. The patient carries a total white blood cell count of 10,200/mm3, including 70% neutrophils, 30% lymphocytes, and zero eosinophils.The erythrocyte sedimentation rate is 20 mm/hr (normal, 0–29 mm/hr). What is the most likely diagnosis?
passage: Silent thyroiditis, also known as painless or subacute lymphocytic thyroiditis, is a self-limited inflammatory condition of the thyroid gland characterized by a painless, diffuse goiter and transient thyrotoxicosis. It typically presents with a small, round, non-tender swelling in the anterior neck that gradually enlarges over weeks. Patients often exhibit symptoms of hyperthyroidism such as palpitations, sweating, insomnia, irritability, nervousness, and fatigue, but lack signs of infection like fever or cervical lymphadenopathy. Unlike De Quervain’s thyroiditis, silent thyroiditis is not associated with significant pain, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate, or a preceding viral illness. White blood cell counts remain normal, and there is no eosinophilia. The condition is also distinct from Graves’ disease, as it lacks ophthalmopathy, pretibial myxedema, and a history of autoimmunity. Silent thyroiditis most commonly occurs in women, often postpartum, and resolves spontaneously within weeks to months, although some patients may develop permanent hypothyroidism. Histologically, it shows lymphocytic infiltration without the fibrosis seen in Hashimoto’s thyroiditis, and thyroid autoantibodies are usually absent.

question: A 27-year-old woman with no past medical history presents to the emergency department with recurrent wrist and neck pain for the last 2 months. She reports that her fingers feel stiff in the morning and the symptoms tend to improve as the day progresses. She denies any recent trauma or other inciting events. Family history is significant for a grandmother who suffered from fibromyalgia. Her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), blood pressure is 120/70 mm Hg, respiratory rate is 16/min, and heart rate is 70/min. On physical examination, her metacarpophalangeal joints are erythematous and tender. Which of the following would be most helpful for diagnosing this patient’s condition?
passage: Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is a chronic autoimmune disorder primarily affecting the synovial joints. It typically presents with symmetric involvement of the small joints of the hands and wrists, morning stiffness lasting more than 30 minutes, and improvement with activity. Physical examination often reveals tender, swollen, and erythematous metacarpophalangeal or proximal interphalangeal joints. Diagnosis is supported by serologic testing; while rheumatoid factor (RF) is commonly associated with RA, it lacks specificity and can be positive in other conditions. The anti–cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibody is highly specific for rheumatoid arthritis and is frequently present early in the disease course, making it a valuable marker for confirming the diagnosis. Synovial fluid analysis can help exclude septic arthritis or crystal arthropathies but is not diagnostic for RA. Imaging studies such as X-rays may show erosions or periarticular osteopenia in later stages, but early RA often lacks radiographic changes. Therefore, anti-CCP antibody testing is particularly useful in patients with typical clinical features and a family history of autoimmune disorders.

question:
A 66-year-old man comes to the physician because of fatigue and swelling of his legs and feet for 6 weeks. During this time he has felt like his eyes have been unusually puffy in the mornings and his urine has been frothy. He has hypertension and rheumatoid factor-positive rheumatoid arthritis. Current medications include amlodipine, methotrexate, and ibuprofen. He does not smoke. He has a history of chronic alcohol abuse. His temperature is 36.7°C (98°F), pulse is 80/min, and blood pressure is 148/86 mm Hg. Physical examination shows pallor, periorbital edema, 2+ pedal edema, and ascites. He has swan-neck deformities and nodules of multiple fingers and swelling and restricted range of motion of bilateral knee joints. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin 8.2 mg/dl
Prothrombin time 12 seconds
Serum
Albumin 2.8 g/dl
Total bilirubin 1.0 mg/dl
Alkaline phosphatase 120 U/L
AST 20 U/L
ALT 19 U/L
Urea 18 mg/dl
Creatinine 1.2 mg/dl
Urine
Protein 3+
WBCs 5–10/hpf
RBCs negative
Bacteria negative
Ultrasound of the liver and kidneys shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's findings?"
passage: Secondary (AA) amyloidosis is a systemic disorder characterized by the extracellular deposition of amyloid fibrils derived from serum amyloid A (SAA), an acute-phase reactant produced by the liver in response to chronic inflammation. Conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis, inflammatory bowel disease, and chronic infections can lead to persistently elevated SAA levels. Over time, the excess SAA protein misfolds and aggregates, depositing in tissues including the kidneys, liver, spleen, and adrenal glands. Renal involvement typically presents with proteinuria, often in the nephrotic range, leading to fatigue, peripheral and periorbital edema, and frothy urine. Hypoalbuminemia and hyperlipidemia may accompany the nephrotic syndrome. Unlike AL amyloidosis, which is associated with plasma cell dyscrasias, AA amyloidosis results from sustained inflammatory states. The diagnosis is confirmed by tissue biopsy showing Congo red-positive amyloid deposits that lose birefringence after potassium permanganate treatment, distinguishing them from AL type. Management focuses on aggressively treating the underlying inflammatory disease to reduce SAA production, thereby slowing or halting amyloid deposition and preserving organ function.

question: A 35-year-old woman presents with an unsteady and broad-based gait, dysmetria, and intention tremor in the distal upper extremities. Her past medical history is relevant for depression, personality changes, and declining work performance, for which she has been put on leave from her current job as an elementary school teacher. On physical examination, patient has dysarthria, dystonia, and an ataxic gait. Ophthalmologic examination reveals multi-colored irises with concentric rings around the periphery (findings shown in the photo). Sensation and motor functions are preserved. Which of the following would you expect to be abnormally accumulated in this patient’s tissues?
passage: Wilson disease is an autosomal recessive disorder of copper metabolism characterized by pathological accumulation of copper in various tissues, particularly the liver, brain, and corneas.
